entries.content: I felt pressured into pursuing medicine even though deep down I didn't really want to. The idea of pursuing medicine wasn't my own.

I'm afraid of what the biggest things in the past to become a doctor it takes at least 10 years from pre med to medical school residency to board exams almost 10 years of intense studying and that's still before actual appointment.
I don't want to still be financially dependent on my parents at that stage. I don't want that she carrying me and it should be kind of encouraged.
There are the stories we always hear about my residents busy birthdays, choosing hospitality over family events, being unable to be physically present while in challenges in their lives.

So one day I finally asked my parents why [they wanted me to pursue medicine]. It was because we wanted to have a stable community career even after you pass which is true without a doubt being in healthcare is one of the most stable in demand.

A student reflects on taking their boisterous, joyful family home for granted, realizing only after entering college and seeing friends and family move away that the simple moments of laughter and conversation are what make life worth living.

What makes my life worth living are those simple moments... It is not always about the individual milestones that make my life moving, but rather any small pieces of joy which I improve everyday living.
The noise that once overwhelmed me now feels precious because it reminds me that I was not alone.
I realized that what I truly long for the future is not success in the traditional sense, but permanent joy.
You can kiss your family and friends goodbye and go miles to see you. At the same time you carry them with you... Because you've not just lived in a world, but a world that's true.
If change is inevitable and distance is unavoidable, what gives life meaning? Making our effort to make room for joy again.
The whole point is to find joy in whatever configuration of life we find ourselves in.
Our desires always remind us of our existential lack or our existential incompleteness. They remind us of how fragmented we really are.
Passion, driven by desire, can be egocentric. It becomes dangerous when our ego is the ultimate reason for what we do.
The command to 'love your neighbor as yourself' implies you must first truly love yourself. Otherwise, love for a neighbor can become an ego project or people-pleasing to prove you are lovable.
I am not sure if this is a retreat from responsible engagement or a search for a transcendence of that macabre nightmare described in Archibald MacLeish’s poem “The End of the World.”

entries.content: Then, at age twenty-six, I came home one night after work and turned on the television set. After graduation, I had taken a job with General Electric but was beginning to grow dissatisfied with the work. After six years of working late at night and on the weekends, I had also started to develop stress-related stomach problems and was wondering how much more I could take.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: On television that night was a documentary about Thomas Merton, a man who had turned his back on a dissolute life to enter a Trappist monastery in the early 1940s. Something about the expression on his face spoke to me: his countenance radiated a peace that to me seemed unknown, or at least forgotten. The show was so interesting that the next day I purchased and began reading Merton's autobiography, The Seven Storey Mountain. Gradually, I discovered within myself a desire to do something similar to what Thomas Merton had done; maybe not join a monastery (since I'm too talkative) but somehow lead a more contemplative, more religious, life.

topics.summary:
This topic explores the fundamental role of desire in the human experience, presenting it as a core aspect of our being that stems from an 'existential lack' or incompleteness. We are defined as 'desiring beings,' constantly reminded of what we don't have, can't fully have, or used to have. This inherent sense of longing is not presented as a flaw but as the primary engine that motivates us to move beyond our comfort zones, seek meaning, and grow throughout our lives.

The course emphasizes the dual nature of desire, using the analogy of fire to illustrate its power. Like fire, desire is a powerful force that is not inherently good or bad, but can be either creative and life-giving or destructive. We are often people of conflicting desires, and our egos can play tricks on us, distorting what we truly want. For example, a seemingly good desire like self-preservation becomes negative when it harms others, and love can be distorted into obsession or overdependence. The central challenge, therefore, is not to suppress desire but to learn how to discern, understand, and channel it in constructive ways.

From a theological, specifically Ignatian, perspective, desire is paramount to the spiritual life. It is the force that can lead us to God. The course posits that God desires a relationship with us, and our own deepest, most authentic desires—for peace, for love, for meaning—can serve as a pathway to discovering this divine relationship. The example of Thomas Merton illustrates how witnessing another's spiritual peace can awaken a profound desire for a more contemplative life, showing that our desires, when properly understood, guide us toward a fuller, more meaningful existence connected to the sacred.
keyTakeaways: Desire is fundamental to the human condition, arising from our 'existential incompleteness' or feeling of lack.
Desire is a powerful, neutral force, often compared to fire. It can be either creative and life-giving or destructive.
The primary spiritual and personal challenge is not to eliminate desire, but to discern and channel it in positive, life-giving ways.
In the Ignatian tradition, our deepest and most authentic desires are a key pathway to finding God, who also desires a relationship with us.
We are often pulled by conflicting desires, and external pressures or our own ego can distort our true longings, leading to inauthentic choices.
studyTips: Focus on the 'Fire' Analogy: Be able to explain why desire is like fire. An exam question might ask you to apply this analogy to a real-life scenario, like ambition or romantic love, explaining how it can both 'warm' (create) and 'burn' (destroy).

Master the 'Creative vs. Destructive' distinction. Use the course diagram (+ over -) as a mental shortcut. For any given desire (e.g., success, security, love), practice listing how it could manifest creatively and destructively.

Prepare for application questions. Review the examples of the medical student, Thomas Merton, and the student missing their family. For each one, be ready to explain what it illustrates about desire (e.g., conflicting desires, authentic spiritual desire, taking things for granted).

Connect Desire to 'Finding God'. This is the core theological point. Memorize the Ignatian idea that 'God desires to be in relationship with us' and our own desires can lead us there. This is a likely essay or short answer question.

Create a 'Distortions' Chart: Make a two-column chart. In one column, list a good human experience or desire (e.g., Love, Self-Preservation, Success). In the second column, list its potential distortions mentioned in the course (e.g., Obsession/Overdependence, Harming others, Perfectionism).

connections.explanation: Our complex and often conflicting desires are not a 'problem' to be solved, but a 'mystery' to be engaged with. Understanding this helps us approach the ambiguity of our own hearts with patience and curiosity rather than frustration.

If we have a distorted image of God (e.g., as a critical accountant), it will poison our desires related to faith. We will desire to hide or perform for God, rather than seek an authentic relationship, which is the ultimate goal of Ignatian desire.

Perfectionism is a prime example of a good desire (the desire to do well) becoming distorted and destructive. It highlights the fine line between a creative impulse and one that 'kills' rather than 'nourishes'.

Our unique set of desires often points us toward a specific spiritual path. A desire for justice points to the Path of Activism, while a desire for awe in nature points to the Path of Creation, showing how desire directs our spiritual energy.

quizQuestions.question: According to the core lessons, what fundamental aspect of the human condition gives rise to desire?
choices: A) Our existential incompleteness or 'lack'
B) The constant influence of social media
C) A biological imperative for survival
D) A theological punishment for sin
quizQuestions.explanation: The course explicitly states that our desires remind us of our 'existential lack' or 'existential incompleteness,' making this the foundational source.

quizQuestions.question: The course uses fire as an analogy for desire. What does this analogy primarily illustrate?
choices: A) That desire is always dangerous and should be extinguished.
B) That desire provides warmth and comfort to our souls.
C) That desire is a powerful, neutral force that can be either creative or destructive.
D) That desire spreads rapidly from one person to another.
quizQuestions.explanation: The fire analogy is used to show desire's dual potential. It is a powerful force that isn't inherently good or bad, but its effects can be, just as fire can be used to cook food (creative) or burn down a house (destructive).

quizQuestions.question: A student feels immense pressure from their family to become a doctor, but their 'deep down' desire is to pursue a different career. This scenario best illustrates which concept from the course?
choices: A) The desire for self-preservation
B) The experience of conflicting desires and external pressures
C) The desire for a contemplative life
D) The distortion of love into obsession
quizQuestions.explanation: This example, taken directly from the materials, shows a conflict between an externally imposed desire (medicine) and an internal, authentic one, highlighting the struggle with conflicting desires.

quizQuestions.question: From an Ignatian perspective, what is the ultimate, positive role of our deepest and most authentic desires?
choices: A) To achieve financial independence and success
B) To help us overcome our ego
C) To lead us into a relationship with God
D) To prove that we are morally superior to others
quizQuestions.explanation: The Ignatian way is founded on the belief that God desires a relationship with us, and our own true desires are a primary means by which we can discover and respond to God's invitation.

quizQuestions.question: The course materials state that self-preservation is a good desire. When does it become a 'bad' or destructive desire?
choices: A) When it requires more than 10 years of study
B) When it comes at a cost to other people's welfare
C) When it leads to financial dependence
D) When it is not your own idea
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'DISTINCTIONS' section makes this point clearly: 'self-preservation is bad if it comes with a cost to other people's welfare.' This highlights the ethical dimension of channeling desire.

quizQuestions.question: The example of Thomas Merton, who saw a documentary and then read an autobiography, illustrates how...
choices: A) Desire can be sparked by an external encounter, leading to a new life path.
B) Joining a monastery is the only way to find peace.
C) Television is a spiritually valuable medium.
D) All desires are ultimately for a more religious life.
quizQuestions.explanation: The narrator's encounter with Merton's story sparked a new desire within them for a 'more contemplative, more religious, life,' showing how our desires can be awakened by external influences and lead us in new directions.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course, obsession and overdependence are distortions of what fundamental human desire or experience?
choices: A) Ambition
B) Wonder
C) Faith
D) Love
quizQuestions.explanation: The materials explicitly list obsession and overdependence as examples of how the powerful and good experience of love can be distorted into something destructive.

quizQuestions.question: The provided list of 14 personal goals (e.g., 'Baki strength,' 'Graduate ME,' 'Retire & Play atoge forever') is used to illustrate that...
choices: A) We should only have one clear desire in life.
B) Our desires are often numerous, varied, and even conflicting.
C) Japanese culture is a major source of modern desires.
D) All desires are ultimately selfish.
quizQuestions.explanation: The wide-ranging and somewhat contradictory nature of the list (e.g., intense physical training vs. retiring to play games) perfectly illustrates the lesson that 'We are people of conflicting desires' and 'fragmented'.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary challenge regarding desire presented in the course?
choices: A) To eliminate all worldly desires and focus only on the spiritual.
B) To satisfy every desire as it arises to achieve happiness.
C) To rank your desires in order of importance.
D) To channel desire in creative and life-giving ways.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course explicitly states, 'The challenge is how to channel desire in creative and life-giving ways,' positioning discernment and direction, not elimination, as the key task.

quizQuestions.question: The course states, 'Because we desire things beyond the tangible & measurable, we love souls.' What does this imply?
choices: A) Physical attraction is not a valid form of desire.
B) Human desire is unique because it is not limited to material objects.
C) Only religious people are capable of true love.
D) Loving souls is easier than desiring tangible things.
quizQuestions.explanation: This statement connects our capacity for abstract desire—for things like meaning, connection, and goodness—to our ability to love another person's inner self ('soul'), suggesting human desire transcends the purely physical or measurable world.

topics.title: Problem vs. Mystery

glossaryTerms.definition: A mystery is an 'inviter' that draws a person into further understanding; its meaning is inexhaustible.
A problem is a mental gap that seeks a solution.
In a theological context, to 'understand' means to grasp what you know, which is not fully possible with God.
To understand means you are able to grasp something within the confines of what you know.

entries.content: No matter how corny it is, it does point to a truth. And the truth is that maybe it's good to examine the reason behind my life motions. The reason behind the motions. Why am I doing what I'm doing?

Let's start with the first Latin word, mysterium. When he talks about mysterium, you're talking about something that's beyond your imagination. Something that's always full. Something that's always so meaningful. For Rudolph Otto, experiencing the sacred is experiencing something completely other from yourself.

When you fully know something... it's very easy to take it for granted and discard it. But when you're dealing with something that you can't really understand, somehow it really captures your attention.
Augustine also said: 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus'—'If you understand, if you can categorize, if you can pin down, maybe it's not God.'
The point of theology is not to understand God, but to misunderstand God less. It's about getting rid of misconceptions.
The problem of 'problem vs. mystery' arises when you take the disposition of problem-solving to a situation that does not call for a clear-cut solution but calls you to be more present and open.

There in the starless dark, the poise, the hover,
There with vast wings across the cancelled skies,
There in the sudden blackness, the sudden pall
Of nothing, nothing, nothing—nothing at all.

entries.content: In religious and perhaps aesthetic terms, mystery refers to things that cannot be proved or disproved but that nevertheless exert a force on our thinking and our lives.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Examples of mystery include: why, for example, there is anything and not nothing; why matter is relatively consistent in its behavior; why suffering and injustice persist; why life is never quite enough; what follows death.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: What, though, has all this to do with the business of the busy world? Is our world not already sufficiently turbulent without dropping “mystery” into the smoky cauldron? And has not science, once and for all, roasted the old witch over the Bunsen burner?
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Mystery requires not understanding so much as engagement—an existential reorienting of self toward what is not known but intuited.

entries.content: A Looming Shadow

entries.content: It is rather strange that half a millennium after Galileo and Newton and, in our own times, Edison, Einstein, Henry Ford and the United Nations, we still find people in poverty, we are still distressed over the loss of everything from youth to global security, still haunted by the tangible nightmares of crime, terrorism and human torture; still dismayed by our growing economic disparity and dwarfed in the looming shadow of our inadequacy to do much about it.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Most of all, we are still humbled in that bone-house of memory where our brightest and most noble efforts to end war and injustice lie, a mockery of their rhetoric, amid the footnotes to our failures. We are compelled to ask, “What have we done wrong—or failed to do?”
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Anna’s King of Siam says, “Is a puzzlement.” The mystic, “It’s a mystery.”
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Mystery, on the other hand, requires not understanding so much as engagement—an existential reorienting of self toward what is not known but intuited...
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Our failures to end war, injustice, poverty and violence have come at a time in the evolution of the cosmos that is most manifest, today, in both the blessings and the destructive capacities our technologies have made possible.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: As catastrophes of the practical imagination, of humanistic idealism unrestrained by historical memory, these failures urge upon us the truth that our institutions, resolutions and technological wonders have failed us and will continue to do so.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Jacque had lived in the same dorm with Brad and me during freshman year. Though wildly different from Brad in outlook and interests, the two became close. After an accounting class one day, standing in a snowfall outside of our old freshman dorm, I told Jacque how angry I was at God, and how I had decided I would no longer go to church. My comments were flung at her like a challenge. You're the believer, I thought, explain this.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: "Well," she said softly, “I've been thanking God for Brad's life.”
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: I can still remember standing in the cold and having my breath taken away by her answer. Rather than arguing about suffering, she was telling me that there were other ways to relate to God, ways other than as the Great Problem Solver.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: She hadn't answered my question about suffering. Rather, her words reminded me that the question of suffering (or the 'mystery of evil' as theologians say) is not the only question to ask about God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: It also reminded me that there are other questions that are equally important—such as “Who is God?" Not being able to answer one question does not mean that others are not equally valid. Her answer opened a window onto another vista of faith.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: That's not to say I ever found an entirely satisfying answer for the mystery of suffering—or for why my friend's life was ended at twenty-one. But it helped me understand the importance of being in relationship with God, even during difficult times.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

topics.summary:
This topic introduces a crucial distinction between a 'problem' and a 'mystery' to frame our approach to theological questions, especially the question of God. A 'problem' is defined as a mental gap that requires a technique to find a clear-cut solution, allowing us to take control and eliminate the gap. In contrast, a 'mystery' is a reality that is inexhaustibly meaningful and invites us into deeper engagement and presence. It's not something to be solved, but rather a reality to be entered into. The meaning of a mystery unfolds more as we commit to it, but it is never fully exhausted.

The course argues that a common error in modern thought is to treat mysteries as if they were problems. We apply a problem-solving disposition to situations like suffering, love, or the meaning of life, which leads to frustration because these realities do not have simple, technical solutions. The text notes that despite centuries of scientific and technological progress, deep existential anxieties persist, highlighting the failure of a purely problem-solving approach to address the human condition. Approaching God is the ultimate example of this distinction. God is not a problem to be figured out or a puzzle to be solved.

Ultimately, the theological takeaway is that the goal is not to 'understand' God in the sense of grasping or controlling the concept, but to 'misunderstand God less' by shedding misconceptions. Quoting Augustine, 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' (If you understand, it is not God), the course emphasizes that God is a mystery that calls for an 'existential reorienting of self.' This reorientation moves us away from seeking definitive answers and towards a disposition of encounter, relationship, and wonder, particularly in the face of what we cannot fully comprehend.
keyTakeaways: A 'problem' is a gap in knowledge that can be solved with a technique, whereas a 'mystery' is an inexhaustibly meaningful reality that invites ongoing engagement.
God is the ultimate mystery, not a problem to be solved. The goal of theology is not to 'understand' God completely, but to 'misunderstand God less' by removing misconceptions.
Applying a problem-solving mindset to a mystery (e.g., trying to find a simple 'answer' for suffering) is a fundamental error that leads to frustration and a distorted view of God.
The proper response to a mystery is not a technique but a disposition of presence, commitment, and wonder. One gets to know more about a mystery by entering into relationship with it.
Engaging a mystery requires an 'existential reorienting of self' toward what is intuited but not fully known, rather than attempting to gain intellectual control over it.
studyTips: Create a T-chart with 'Problem' on one side and 'Mystery' on the other. For each term, list its associated words from the notes: (Problem: solution, technique, control, solved, mental gap) vs. (Mystery: engagement, inviter, inexhaustible, encounter, presence, wonder, relationship). This will help you quickly distinguish them on an exam.

Memorize and be able to explain Augustine's quote: 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus.' It is the key to the entire topic. An exam will likely ask you to explain how this quote relates to the problem/mystery distinction.

Practice applying the concept. Use the example of Brad's death from the notes. Ask yourself: What was the 'problem' the narrator was trying to solve? How did Jacque's response reframe it as a 'mystery'? Be ready to analyze a new scenario using this framework.

Connect the two dispositions toward mystery—'Absolute Certainty' and 'Wonder'—to this topic. Recognize that treating a mystery as a problem is a form of seeking 'Absolute Certainty,' while the correct response is 'Wonder.'

Focus on the specific theological definition of 'understand': to grasp something within the confines of what you know. Be prepared to explain why this definition makes 'understanding' God impossible and reinforces God's status as a mystery.

connections.explanation: Wonder is the proper disposition when facing a mystery. While a problem leads to a solution, a mystery should evoke wonder, which fuels ongoing engagement rather than closing off inquiry.

Viewing God as a 'problem' to be solved or an entity who must provide clear-cut answers on demand is a prime example of a 'Distorted Image of God.' Understanding God as a mystery helps correct this by emphasizing relationship over transaction.

Faith is not about possessing all the answers (a 'solution'), but about trusting and committing to a relationship in the midst of what is not fully known. Faith is the act of engaging with the mystery of God.

Experiencing the 'Sacred,' as described by Rudolph Otto, means encountering something 'completely other' than yourself. This experience aligns with the concept of mystery, as it is something beyond our full comprehension that we encounter rather than solve.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is the fundamental difference between a 'problem' and a 'mystery'?
choices: A) A problem is difficult to solve, while a mystery is impossible to solve.
B) A problem seeks a clear-cut solution through a technique, while a mystery invites deeper engagement and has inexhaustible meaning.
C) A problem is a scientific question, while a mystery is a religious question.
D) A problem is a mental gap, while a mystery is an emotional feeling.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core distinction is in the required response. A problem calls for a solution that closes the knowledge gap. A mystery, being inexhaustibly meaningful, calls for ongoing engagement and presence.

quizQuestions.question: The student's anger at God after his friend Brad's death, and his challenge to Jacque to 'explain this,' is an example of what?
choices: A) A healthy expression of faith.
B) Applying the disposition of 'Wonder' to a difficult situation.
C) Approaching the mystery of suffering as if it were a problem demanding a solution.
D) A successful use of a theological technique to find peace.
quizQuestions.explanation: The student's demand for an explanation treats the tragedy of suffering as a 'problem' that his 'believer' friend should be able to 'solve.' This illustrates the core error the topic warns against.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary implication of Augustine's quote, 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' (If you understand, it is not God)?
choices: A) God is deliberately confusing and wants to remain unknown.
B) Only the most intelligent theologians can truly understand God.
C) Any concept of God that can be fully grasped or defined by the human mind is by definition a limited idol, not God.
D) Understanding God is the first step toward true faith.
quizQuestions.explanation: The quote means that God, as an infinite mystery, cannot be contained within the finite limits of human comprehension ('to grasp what you know'). If you think you've fully figured God out, what you've figured out is a creation of your own mind, not God.

quizQuestions.question: Which statement best reflects the course's perspective on the goal of theology?
choices: A) To prove the existence of God through logical arguments.
B) To achieve a complete and certain understanding of God's nature.
C) To categorize all of God's attributes in a systematic way.
D) To misunderstand God less by clearing away misconceptions and entering into relationship.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course explicitly states, 'The point of theology is not to understand God, but to misunderstand God less. It's about getting rid of misconceptions.' This aligns with approaching God as a mystery.

quizQuestions.question: How is a 'mystery' described in the course materials?
choices: A) A mental gap that seeks a resolution which leads someone to take control.
B) An 'inviter' that draws a person into further understanding because its meaning is inexhaustible.
C) A complex situation that has not yet been solved by science.
D) A truth that can be proved but is difficult to accept.
quizQuestions.explanation: The definition used multiple times is that a mystery is an 'inviter' whose meaning is 'inexhaustible.' Choice A describes a 'problem.'

quizQuestions.question: The course suggests that despite advancements from Galileo to Henry Ford, we still face nightmares like crime, poverty, and terrorism. What is the reason given for this failure?
choices: A) We haven't invested enough money in technological solutions.
B) Human nature is inherently evil and cannot be changed.
C) These are mysteries that cannot be solved by applying the problem-solving logic of technology and institutions alone.
D) The United Nations has been ineffective in its mission.

topics.summary:
This topic explores the critical role of doubt in a mature theological understanding, arguing against the pursuit of 'Absolute Certainty.' The central thesis is that absolute certainty is a mental trap, often stemming from a desire for control and leading to reductionism, dogmatism, and dangerous polarization. The course posits that we should avoid absolute certainty because human knowledge is inherently limited and finite, a concept supported by Charles Sanders Peirce's doctrine of 'Fallibilism.'

As a constructive alternative, the lesson champions 'Creative Doubt,' defined as the active process of challenging and evaluating one's core beliefs. Four key arguments are presented in its favor: 1) it healthily acknowledges our human limitations (finitude); 2) it keeps our beliefs alive and dynamic; 3) it acts as a safeguard against turning our beliefs into idols (idolatry); and 4) avoiding it can lead to a 'dehumanizing quest for certainty,' where freedom is sacrificed for comfort, as illustrated by Dostoevsky's 'Grand Inquisitor.'

Finally, the material warns against two common but flawed responses to mystery that arise from a desire for certainty: Fundamentalism (a rigid adherence to doctrines) and Relativism (the belief that truth is merely individual). Both are forms of reductionism that fail to engage with the complexity of truth. The ultimate goal, as Bertrand Russell suggests, is to learn how to live without absolute certainty, yet without being paralyzed by hesitation, thereby embracing both intellectual humility and meaningful commitment.
keyTakeaways: Absolute certainty is presented as a 'mental trap' and a 'dehumanizing quest' driven by a desire for control, which leads to reductionism and deadly polarization.

Creative Doubt is a valuable theological tool. The four arguments in its defense are: it acknowledges human finitude, keeps beliefs vital, is a weapon against idolatry, and its avoidance can mean relinquishing freedom.
Fallibilism (C.S. Peirce) is the doctrine that humans are always fallible in matters of fact and can never achieve absolute certainty. Our knowledge 'swims in a continuum of uncertainty.'
Fundamentalism and Relativism are two opposing but equally flawed reductionist responses to mystery. Fundamentalism reduces truth to rigid rules, while Relativism reduces it to the individual.

The avoidance of doubt is a rejection of freedom. As seen in 'The Grand Inquisitor,' people may desire to lay down the 'terrible gift' of freedom for the comfort of being told what to believe with certainty.
studyTips: Focus on the 'why' behind the arguments. Don't just memorize that certainty is bad; be able to explain *why* it's linked to control, reductionism, and polarization, using examples like Bronowski at Auschwitz.

Create a 'mental traps' chart. List Absolute Certainty, Analysis Paralysis, and Apathetic Avoidance. For each, write a short definition and an example to clearly distinguish how they hinder true thinking.

Master the three key literary/historical examples. Be ready to explain how the Socratic method in 'Euthyphro,' Dostoevsky's 'Grand Inquisitor,' and Bronowski at Auschwitz each illustrate a core concept of this topic. Exam questions often ask you to apply these.

Distinguish between Fundamentalism and Relativism. Know their definitions and understand how *both* are presented as failed attempts to achieve certainty and escape the complexity of mystery, though they do so in opposite ways.
Practice connecting the key quotes to the core concepts. For example, link Cromwell's 'think it possible you may be mistaken' to Fallibilism and the value of creative doubt.

connections.explanation: The pursuit of absolute certainty is an attempt to reduce a 'Mystery' (a reality we are part of and cannot fully grasp) into a 'Problem' (something external that can be solved and controlled). Creative doubt is a way of living within the mystery.

The course explicitly contrasts the pursuit of certainty with wonder. Certainty closes down inquiry ('I already know'), while wonder opens us up to new possibilities, which is the very function of creative doubt.

The course states, 'Avoid absolute certainty and perfectionism.' Both stem from an inability to accept human finitude. The desire for a perfect, unassailable belief system is a theological form of perfectionism.

Believing in a God who demands absolute, unthinking certainty can be a 'distorted image of God.' This topic suggests that a mature faith involves grappling with doubt, rather than adhering to a rigid, fundamentalist image of a God who punishes questions.

quizQuestions.explanation: The Inquisitor argues that people willingly trade their freedom for the certainty provided by 'miracle, mystery and authority,' which directly corresponds to the course's argument that avoiding doubt can be a dehumanizing sacrifice of freedom.

choices: A) Relativism
B) Reductionism

quizQuestions.explanation: Bronowski connects the horrors of Auschwitz to what happens when people are dogmatically certain they are right, illustrating the 'deadly polarization' that can result from thinking in absolutes.

choices: A) Creative Doubt
B) Socratic Method
C) Reductionism
D) Fallibilism
quizQuestions.explanation: Both Fundamentalism (reducing truth to rigid rules) and Relativism (reducing truth to the individual) are presented as reductionist perspectives attached to the problematic pursuit of certainty.

choices: A) It acknowledges human finitude.
B) It is a necessary step towards achieving absolute truth.
C) It keeps beliefs vital.
D) It is a weapon against idolatry.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course argues against the very possibility and desirability of achieving absolute truth. Creative doubt is a way to live without it, not a path to it. The other three options are explicitly listed as arguments for creative doubt.

quizQuestions.question: A student who is not open to new perspectives because they are rigidly convinced they already have the complete truth is falling into which mental trap?
choices: A) Analysis Paralysis
B) Apathetic Avoidance
C) Absolute Certainty
D) Relativism
quizQuestions.explanation: This is the precise definition of 'Absolute Certainty' as a mental trap provided in the glossary: 'A mental trap where one is not open to new perspectives and is not truly thinking.'

quizQuestions.question: The Socratic dialogue 'Euthyphro,' where Socrates challenges Euthyphro's understanding of piety, is used as an example of:
choices: A) The problem of relativism
B) The failure of philosophy
C) The practice of creative doubt
D) The dangers of apathetic avoidance
quizQuestions.explanation: Socrates's method of questioning fundamental beliefs, even those held with great confidence, is a classic example of 'creative doubt' in action, challenging someone to re-evaluate their core commitments.

quizQuestions.question: What is identified as the main problem of Relativism?
choices: A) It leads to rigid and deadly polarization.
B) It dissolves shared values.
C) It requires too much intellectual effort.
D) It is a form of idolatry.
quizQuestions.explanation: The glossary explicitly defines the 'Relativism Problem' as 'the dissolving of shared values,' which occurs when truth is seen as entirely dependent on the individual with no objective basis for community.

quizQuestions.question: Bertrand Russell's statement about learning to 'live without certainty, and yet without being paralyzed by hesitation' best describes the balance between which two extremes?
choices: A) Fundamentalism and Relativism
B) Faith and Reason
C) Absolute Certainty and Analysis Paralysis
D) Desire and Wonder
quizQuestions.explanation: Russell's quote captures the ideal middle path. 'Live without certainty' is the rejection of Absolute Certainty, while not being 'paralyzed by hesitation' is the avoidance of Analysis Paralysis. The goal is to act meaningfully despite not knowing everything.

topics.summary:
This topic explores the concept of 'complementarities and paradoxes,' which are ideas that seem contradictory but, when held together, reveal a deeper and richer truth. A central theme is that life, and our understanding of God, cannot be reduced to simple 'YES or NO' binaries. The primary theological paradox discussed is God's nature as being simultaneously Transcendent (beyond our existence and experience) and Immanent (within our existence and experience). This is beautifully captured in the phrase 'more inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest.'

This principle extends beyond theology into human experience. Examples like falling in love (being both thrilling and uncomfortable), a loving person being both gentle and firm, and the necessary tension between tradition and prophecy all illustrate this concept. Rembrandt's painting 'Return of the Prodigal Son,' with the father's dual masculine and feminine hands, serves as a powerful visual metaphor for this kind of complementary truth. The course emphasizes that we must learn to 'find life in the tension' rather than trying to resolve these paradoxes into simple answers.

Ultimately, the goal is to develop 'synthetic thinking'—the ability to see connections and hold seemingly opposing ideas together to perceive a more coherent, complex whole. This skill moves us away from a rigid desire for 'Absolute Certainty' and toward a more open disposition of 'Wonder,' allowing us to better navigate the difficulties and complexities of the spiritual life and the world at large.
keyTakeaways: A paradox or complementarity is not a contradiction, but two seemingly opposing truths that, when held together, reveal a richer, more complete reality.

The core theological paradox is that God is both Transcendent (infinitely beyond us) and Immanent (intimately within us). Remember the quote: 'more inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest.'
Synthetic thinking is the skill of seeing the 'bigger picture' by connecting ideas and embracing paradoxes, moving beyond simple binary (YES/NO) logic.
Paradoxes are a fundamental part of human and spiritual life, seen in experiences like love (attraction/discomfort), leadership (gentle/firm), and faith (spirituality/religion).
The goal is not to solve or eliminate these tensions, but to 'live in them,' as they provide a healthy balance and lead to growth and a deeper understanding.
studyTips: Focus on the definition: An exam question will almost certainly ask you to define or identify a paradox. Memorize the key phrase: 'seemingly contradictory, but express a richer truth.'

Use visual aids to remember key pairs. Connect 'Transcendent/Immanent' to the Yin-Yang symbol (chaos/order) or a vertical line dividing a page, representing two sides of a whole. Picture Rembrandt's painting with its 'masculine/feminine' hands to remember God's gentle and firm nature.

Practice 'synthetic thinking' by actively looking for complementarities in other course topics. For example, how is faith both a gift and a choice? How is desire both sacred and dangerous? This will prepare you for synthesis questions.

Distinguish paradox from contradiction. Remember the 'God creating a rock' example is a logical contradiction, not a theological paradox. A paradox enriches understanding of God's nature; a contradiction negates it. Expect a question that tests this distinction.

Prepare for application questions. Don't just memorize definitions. Be ready to explain HOW an example (like falling in love or the tension between tradition and prophetic individuals) illustrates the concept of complementarity.

connections.explanation: The course states that spirituality and religion are a key complementarity; neither can be fully realized without the other. This is a prime example of two distinct concepts forming a richer whole in a person's relationship with God.
Understanding paradox is essential for moving from a mindset of 'Absolute Certainty,' which demands simple, binary answers, to a disposition of 'Wonder,' which can embrace complexity and mystery.

The paradox of God being both gentle (immanent) and firm (transcendent) serves as a corrective to distorted images of God as either 'too weak' or 'too strict.' The complementary view provides a more balanced and complete picture.

The experience of falling in love is used as a primary illustration of a paradox, being both a 'thrilling' attraction and an 'uncomfortable' disruption. This directly links the abstract concept of paradox to the lived, complex experience of human desire.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is the best definition of a paradox or complementarity?
choices: A) Two things that are seemingly contradictory, but when put together, express a richer truth.
B) A philosophical contradiction that demonstrates the limits of logic, like 'Can God create a rock he cannot lift?'.
C) Two opposing forces where one must ultimately be chosen over the other, like truth versus happiness.
D) A problem that has no possible solution and must be accepted as a mystery.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer defines a paradox as revealing a 'richer truth' by holding two seeming contradictions in tension. A is the precise definition from the course. B describes a logical contradiction, not a theological paradox. C describes a conflict, not a complementarity. D is closer to the definition of 'Mystery' but misses the specific structure of a paradox.

C) The relationship between faith and reason.
D) The problem of evil in a world created by a good God.
quizQuestions.explanation: This classic quote from St. Augustine perfectly captures the simultaneous 'higher than' (Transcendence) and 'inward than' (Immanence) nature of God.

quizQuestions.question: How does the analysis of the father's hands in Rembrandt's 'Return of the Prodigal Son' serve as an illustration in this topic?
choices: A) It shows the artist's technical skill and has no theological meaning.
B) It represents the choice between a weak God and a strict God.
C) It symbolizes God's nature as being both gentle (feminine hand) and firm (masculine hand).
D) It demonstrates the father's forgiveness and the son's repentance.
quizQuestions.explanation: The painting is used to illustrate complementarity. The two different hands—one seen as masculine/firm, the other as feminine/gentle—symbolize a complete, paradoxical love that is both strong and tender, correcting one-sided images of God.

quizQuestions.question: The course emphasizes developing 'synthetic thinking'. In the context of this topic, what does this skill enable a student to do?
choices: A) Argue that one side of a paradox is more correct than the other.
B) Create new religious doctrines from scratch.
C) Disregard details in favor of a vague, general feeling.
D) See connections between different ideas to perceive a coherent, complex whole.
quizQuestions.explanation: Synthetic thinking, as defined in the course, is the ability to see the bigger picture by connecting various parts. Embracing paradoxes, rather than being defeated by them, is a key component of this skill.

quizQuestions.question: Why is the question 'Can God create a rock so heavy he cannot lift it?' considered a philosophical contradiction rather than a theological paradox?
choices: A) Because it involves a physical object (a rock) and not a spiritual concept.
B) Because any answer negates God's omnipotent nature, whereas a paradox enriches it.
C) Because it has already been answered by famous theologians.
D) Because it is a question of power, not of love.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course distinguishes between a paradox that reveals a deeper truth about God's nature (e.g., being both gentle and firm) and a logical contradiction that negates God's nature. This question forces a logical contradiction where God is not all-powerful, which is why it's not a theological paradox in this context.

quizQuestions.question: The feeling of falling in love is used as an example of a paradox because it is often experienced as:
choices: A) Purely positive and life-affirming.
B) A temporary emotion that fades over time.
C) Both a thrilling attraction and an uncomfortable disruption.
D) A logical decision rather than an emotional one.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course material highlights the tension in falling in love: the attraction is thrilling, but it also 'bothers your usual way of going about things,' creating a feeling of discomfort. This pairing of attraction and discomfort is the paradox.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following pairs is presented in the course as a key complementarity in a healthy spiritual life?
choices: A) Spirituality and Religion
B) Truth and Happiness
C) Order and Chaos
D) All of the above
quizQuestions.explanation: All these pairs are used to illustrate complementarity. A) 'Neither can be fully realized without the other.' B) The Socrates vs. Emmanuel conflict highlights the tension between truth and contentment. C) The Yin-Yang symbol is used to represent the necessary interplay of order and chaos.

quizQuestions.question: The course suggests a tension between 'religious traditions' and 'prophetic individuals' is healthy because:
choices: A) Prophetic individuals eventually create their own, better traditions.
B) Tradition provides a corrective to individual arrogance, while prophets challenge institutional stagnation.
C) Religious traditions are always right and prophets are usually mistaken.
D) The conflict proves that one must choose either the institution or the individual.
quizQuestions.explanation: This is a key example of a healthy, life-giving tension. The institution (tradition) provides stability and wisdom, while the individual (prophet) provides a necessary push for change and growth, preventing complacency.

quizQuestions.question: Which concept is contrasted with 'Wonder' as a way of approaching the world?
choices: A) Absolute Certainty
B) Synthetic Thinking
C) Immanence
D) Faith
quizQuestions.explanation: The course draws a contrast between a disposition of 'Wonder,' which is open to paradox and mystery, and a disposition of 'Absolute Certainty,' which seeks rigid, binary answers and is uncomfortable with tension.

quizQuestions.question: The term 'immanent' specifically refers to God's nature as being:
choices: A) Beyond our experience.
B) Within our experience.
C) All-powerful and all-knowing.
D) Unchanging and eternal.
quizQuestions.explanation: The glossary and definitions explicitly define 'immanent' as 'To be within our experience.' Its complement, 'transcendent,' is defined as 'To be beyond our experience.'

Who here likes the ocean? ... Why is that? ... It's very interesting that the way that you phrase why you like one other thing is because you don't like what the other thing has... more positively. Why is the beach a favorite for you?
My guess is that if you tend to be more introverted, you probably like the mountains more. If you're more extroverted, you'd probably like to beach more. That's just my guess.
What is the point of building towards a stable future if I lose it? What is the point of earning more money as I risk the moment of happiness?
Because money can always be earned again, opportunities can return, but I'm never going to be 18 again.
Because we are so busy chasing the next moments in our lives we need to start realizing that we don't need more, we just need to be more present in the life that we already have.
Maybe it's not about how impressive your life looks so big. Maybe it's about how deep you love and how message you bring to the people.
Because at the end of our lives people... will remember if you were there.
College is not meant to be endured. It's meant to be enjoyed. A lot of the core memories that we safely have come from the moments we enjoy.

Metanoia is a change in perspective, moving from an over-reliance on an empirical view to an openness to mystery. It is not throwing away structures of knowledge, but opening up their scope.

topics.summary:
This topic explores 'Wonder' as a crucial theological and human disposition, contrasting it with the modern over-reliance on empirical knowledge and a constant drive for future achievement. The central argument is that our society is so focused on gaining more—more money, more success, more data—that we've lost the ability to be present in our lives. This has led to a 'betrayal of our sense of mystery,' where we reduce complex human experiences like love and tragedy to measurable or strategic problems. The proposed solution is a 'metanoia,' or a change in perspective, which involves embracing mystery and re-engaging our imagination.

The course posits that art, music, and literature offer a distinct way of knowing that is ontological and intuitional, rather than purely conceptual or informational. Through the 'aesthetic imagination,' we can achieve a deep resonance with the world that science and logic alone cannot provide. This allows for a universal response to human experience, connecting us to deeper truths that we can intuit but not always rationally prove. This shift in perspective is not about abandoning reason, but expanding its scope to include the unknown and the mysterious.

Practically, cultivating wonder is not a passive state but an active disposition that requires two key attitudes: humility and commitment. Humility is defined as truthfulness—knowing one's strengths and weaknesses—and is etymologically linked to the earth ('humus'), suggesting a groundedness in reality. Commitment involves intention and structure, creating the conditions for wonder to grow, much like a tree must remain planted in one spot to mature. Ultimately, by recovering our sense of wonder, we can 'become as little children,' opening ourselves up to growth, surprise, and a deeper, more present engagement with life, which is presented as the foundation for art, science, and religion.
keyTakeaways: The core problem addressed is our modern tendency to chase future moments at the expense of being present, leading to a loss of meaning and happiness.
Metanoia is a key concept: a change in perspective from an over-reliance on empirical data to an openness to mystery, which expands our ways of knowing.
Wonder is an active disposition requiring two attitudes: Humility (truthfulness about oneself) and Commitment (intention and structure to facilitate growth).

The 'Aesthetic Imagination' (often through art) provides an ontological or intuitional form of knowledge, allowing a deep 'resonance' with reality that is distinct from conceptual or scientific knowledge.
Recovering our sense of wonder is presented as a solution to a world in crisis, enabling us to change ourselves by re-engaging with the mystery that underlies creation.
studyTips: Focus on defining and distinguishing key terms: 'Wonder' vs. 'Curiosity', 'Conceptual knowledge' vs. 'Ontological knowledge', and 'Practical imagination' vs. 'Aesthetic imagination'. Exam questions will likely test these distinctions.

Memorize the two attitudes of wonder: Humility and Commitment. Be able to explain each one using the definitions and illustrations provided (e.g., Humility = truthfulness, from 'humus'; Commitment = required for growth, like a tree).

Master the concept of 'Metanoia'. You should be able to explain it as a shift from a purely empirical view to one that embraces mystery, and how this relates to the role of art and imagination as alternative ways of knowing.

Connect the 'problem' to the 'solution'. Be prepared to articulate the course's critique of modern life (reducing mystery to problems, longing to market allure) and explain how recapturing wonder is presented as the remedy.

Think about the quotes. An exam might ask you to interpret a quote like John Kavanaugh's 'war is a failure of the imagination' in the context of this lesson. Practice explaining how each quote illustrates a core concept.

connections.explanation: This topic is the direct philosophical framework for Wonder. The lesson argues we must shift from treating life as a series of 'problems' to be solved by reason to embracing 'mystery' through wonder and imagination.

The lesson critiques how modern life reduces 'longing to market allure' and suggests that the practice of wonder, through 'commitment,' helps to properly 'channel desires' away from endless acquisition and towards presence.

Cultivating wonder and an 'openness to mystery' is presented as a prerequisite for spiritual engagement. This sensibility is foundational to faith, which by nature deals with realities beyond empirical proof.

The advice that 'College is not meant to be endured. It's meant to be enjoyed' directly counters a perfectionist mindset. The emphasis on being present over chasing the 'next moment' offers an alternative to the constant pressure for future achievement.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, what is 'metanoia'?
choices: A) A change in perspective from an over-reliance on an empirical view to an openness to mystery.
B) The process of using logic and reason to solve complex theological problems.
C) The feeling of awe one experiences when looking at nature or art.
D) A specific artistic technique for creating resonance with an audience.
quizQuestions.explanation: Metanoia is explicitly defined as a change in perspective, moving beyond the purely empirical to embrace mystery. The other options describe related but incorrect concepts.

quizQuestions.question: The course argues that the communication of art is primarily...
choices: A) Conceptual and informational.
B) Ontological and intuitional.
C) Moral and directive.
D) Strategic and logical.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the communication of art is 'ontological rather than conceptual, intuitional rather than informational,' distinguishing it from scientific or logical discourse.

quizQuestions.question: Which two attitudes are required for the disposition of wonder?
choices: A) Curiosity and skepticism
B) Joy and happiness
C) Humility and commitment
D) Attention and surprise
quizQuestions.explanation: The core lessons explicitly state that 'There are two attitudes associated with wonder: humility and commitment.' While attention and surprise are related to wonder, humility and commitment are presented as the core required attitudes.

quizQuestions.question: The analogy of a tree that doesn't grow if it keeps moving is used to illustrate which concept?
choices: A) The weakness of humility
B) The need for commitment in cultivating wonder
C) The failure of the aesthetic imagination
D) The futility of chasing happiness
quizQuestions.explanation: This illustration is used to show that wonder, like a tree, requires structure and intention to grow. Commitment provides this stability, allowing one to deepen their understanding and perspective.

quizQuestions.question: How does the course distinguish 'wonder' from 'curiosity'?
choices: A) Wonder is a disposition, while curiosity is a desire for knowledge.
B) Wonder is for art, while curiosity is for science.
C) There is no distinction; the terms are used interchangeably.
D) Curiosity leads to mystery, while wonder leads to answers.
quizQuestions.explanation: The definitions provided state that 'Wonder is the disposition of a learner' while 'Curiosity is the desire for knowledge.' This frames wonder as a broader stance or attitude, and curiosity as a more specific drive.

quizQuestions.question: The argument that we have 'betrayed our sense of mystery' criticizes the tendency to...
choices: A) Engage with poetry and art.
B) Reduce complex experiences like tragedy to problems in pathology or strategy.
C) Prioritize being present over earning more money.
D) Become like little children.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text lists examples of this betrayal, such as 'when we reduced... tragedy to pathology and love to sexuality; when we addressed the ageless evils of war and injustice as problems in strategy...'.

quizQuestions.question: What is the role of the 'aesthetic imagination'?
choices: A) To create logical proofs and moral directives from art.
B) To solve strategic challenges in the physical world.
C) To achieve an inner 'resonance' with an object by intently 'attending' to it.
D) To organize sensory data into empirical categories.
quizQuestions.explanation: The definition of aesthetic imagination describes a process of 'becoming' the object by 'attending' to it to achieve an inner 'resonance,' which is an intuitional, not a logical or empirical, process.

quizQuestions.question: The etymology of 'humility' is connected to 'humus' (soil) to suggest that humility is about...
choices: A) Feeling dirty or worthless.
B) A grounded truthfulness about oneself.
C) A desire to return to nature.
D) The intellectual pursuit of agriculture.
quizQuestions.explanation: The connection to 'humus' (earth/soil) emphasizes that humility is a kind of groundedness—a truthfulness about where you are strong and weak, rather than an abstract concept or a feeling of low self-worth.

quizQuestions.question: John Kavanaugh's quote, 'war is a failure of the imagination,' suggests that...
choices: A) Soldiers are not creative enough.
B) War could be avoided if we used our capacity for aesthetic and spiritual engagement to find alternatives to force.
C) Artists and poets should be in charge of military strategy.
D) The logical restrictions of human thought are the only way to prevent war.
quizQuestions.explanation: Within the context of the lesson, imagination is the faculty for grasping mystery and engaging with reality outside of pure logic and strategy. A 'failure of the imagination' means we are stuck in a problem-solving mode (strategy, force) and cannot envision other ways of being or resolving conflict.

quizQuestions.question: The course suggests that in our busy lives, we need to realize that...
choices: A) We need more money to be truly happy.
B) We need to work harder to build a stable future.
C) We don't need more, we just need to be more present.
D) We need more structures of knowledge to understand the world.
quizQuestions.explanation: This is a direct quote from the Core Lessons that captures the central practical takeaway of the topic: the solution is not accumulation ('more') but a shift in attention ('more present').

topics.title: The Six Paths

glossaryTerms.definition: A - Apply: show how your topic plays out in real life.
D - Define: say what the topic means accurately.
E - Explain: talk about the component parts and why it is important.
I - Identify: know what exact topic you're talking about.
S - Synthesize: connect it to other topics and to concepts outside the immediate subject.

For people on this first path, belief in God has always been part of their lives. They were born into religious families or were introduced to religion at an early age. They move through life more or less confident of their belief in God. Faith has always been an essential element of their lives. They pray regularly, attend religious services frequently, and feel comfortable talking about God.

This final path crosses all the other ones at various points. People on the path of confusion run hot and cold with their childhood faith— finding it relatively easy to believe in God at times, almost impossible at others. They haven't 'fallen away,' but they've not stayed connected either. They cry out to God in prayer and then wonder why there doesn't seem to be an answer. They intuit God's presence during important moments, and perhaps even during religious services, but find themselves bothered by the problems of belonging to a church, synagogue, or mosque. They may pray from time to time, particularly when in dire need, and they may go to services on key holidays. But for this group, finding God is a mystery, a worry, or a problem.

Those traveling along the path of disbelief not only find that organized religion holds no appeal (even if they sometimes find its services and rituals comforting), but have also arrived at an intellectual conclusion that God may not, does not, or cannot exist.
My time with the actors was one of not only discovering the theater but also meeting people who were traveling along a path I hadn't encountered before. They were on the path of exploration.
Those on the path of independence have made a conscious decision to separate themselves from organized religion, but they still believe in God.

This path gets more crowded every year. People in this group typically begin life in a religious family but drift away from their faith. After a childhood in which they were encouraged (or forced) to attend religious services, they find them either tiresome or irrelevant or both. Religion remains distant, though oddly appealing.

My guess is that the moment that you reach adolescence... you go through what's called a period of disengagement. It means that you are trying to discover your individual self apart from institutions and pre made structures.

entries.content: Now we go to the six paths. I think it's important to ask, where are you in God?

My guess is that the moment that you reach adolescence... you go through what's called a period of disengagement. It means that you are trying to discover your individual self apart from institutions and pre made structures.

Growing up, for instance, a lot of you... were obliged to go to mass on Sundays... but the moment that you're more conscious about your adult self, you're now starting to discover things a bit more. Maybe you're discovering for instance, your sexuality in a way that perhaps goes against what your religion has taught you.

Remember that where you are on the six paths is largely influenced by the state of your life now. It's not that you can just choose... The choosing is not in what you see in your mind of how you live your life. So pay attention to the trajectory of your life.

The path of exploration talks about an openness to God, but you're trying many different kinds of religious paths and religious practices... In a way you still have a very positive view of religion... whereas the path of confusion has a more negative view of religion because we've seen how for instance, religion has failed people.

[Path of Confusion is like] I believe in God, but I'm starting to really not like religious structures or why do I feel like religious structures are saying one thing but doing another... Am I making sense here? That's the difference. The difference is in how they see or feel.

What is the difference among the meaning of confusion and independence since it both has more of negative view of religion? ...Independence I think has a very clear interest, individual shape... Confusion, you're still sort of at that point where I want to be religious. But there are many things that are discouraging.

Whereas independence, you're very clear, religion is okay. I think there's a lot of good in it, but I want to try things out completely by myself. ...it doesn't necessarily translate into a negative view of religion. It just means that you want... It's similar to exploration... with independence, you're really starting to see less of the value of communal worship.
The Six Paths
