entries.content: The early life of Walter Ciszek, an American Jesuit priest who spent twenty years in Soviet prisons and Siberian labor camps beginning in the 1940s, reflects this kind of upbringing. In his autobiography, With God in Russia, published after his return to the United States, Ciszek describes growing up in a devout Catholic family in the coal belt of Pennsylvania. Family life centered on the local parish: Sunday Masses, special feast days, weekly confessions.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: "It must have been through my mother's prayers and example that I made up my mind in the eighth grade, out of a clear blue sky, that I would be a priest."
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: What for many people would be a difficult decision was for young Walter Ciszek the most natural thing in the world.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The benefits of walking along the path of belief are clear: faith gives meaning to both the joys and struggles of life. Faith in God means that you know that you are never alone. You know and are known. Life within a worshipping community provides companionship. During times of hardship, faith is an anchor. And the Christian faith also holds out the promise of life beyond this earthly one.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This kind of faith sustained Walter Ciszek during his years in the Soviet labor camps and enabled him, as he finally left Russia in 1963, to bless the country whose government had caused him untold physical and mental suffering. At times he struggled with his belief—who wouldn't in such conditions?—but ultimately his faith remained firm.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: With God in Russia ends with these haunting words, describing what Ciszek does as his plane takes off: "Slowly, carefully, I made the sign of the cross over the land I was leaving.”
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: If you envy those on the path of belief, don't worry—many people go through a period of doubt and confusion before they come to know God. Sometimes for a long time.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Ignatius finally accepted God's presence at an age when many of his peers were well on their way to raising a family and achieving financial success.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: None of these six paths is free from dangers. One pitfall for those on the path of belief is an inability to understand people on other paths and a temptation to judge them for their doubt or disbelief.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: There is a more subtle danger for this group: a complacency that makes one's relationship with God stagnate. Some people cling to ways of understanding their faith learned in childhood that might not work for an adult.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Like all of the six paths, the path of belief is not without its stumbling blocks.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Independence
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Those on the path of independence have made a conscious decision to separate themselves from organized religion, but they still believe in God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: They are sometimes called “lapsed,” “fallen away,” or “recovering” Catholics.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Though they keep their distance from churches, synagogues, or mosques, many people in this group are still firm believers. Often they find solace in the religious practices they learned as children. Just as often they long for a more formal way to worship God in their lives.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: One strength of this group is a healthy independence that enables them to see things in a fresh way-something that their own religious community often desperately needs. Those on the 'outside,' who are not bound by the usual restrictions on what is 'appropriate' and 'not appropriate' to say within the community, can often speak more honestly.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: It's also a reminder that for those on the path of independence—believers who have left organized religion—the search for a perfect religious community may be one without end.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Disbelief
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Those traveling along the path of disbelief not only find that organized religion holds no appeal (even if they sometimes find its services and rituals comforting), but have also arrived at an intellectual conclusion that God may not, does not, or cannot exist.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The cardinal benefit of this group is that they take none of the bland reassurances of religion for granted. Sometimes they have thought more deeply about God and religion than some believers have.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: They also have a knack for detecting hypocrisy, cant, or lazy answers: a religious-baloney detector. Tell a person in this group that suffering is part of God's mysterious plan and needs to be accepted unquestioningly, and he will rightly challenge you to explain yourself.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The main danger for this group is that they sometimes expect God's presence to be proven solely in an intellectual way.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Return
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This path gets more crowded every year. People in this group typically begin life in a religious family but drift away from their faith. After a childhood in which they were encouraged (or forced) to attend religious services, they find them either tiresome or irrelevant or both. Religion remains distant, though oddly appealing.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Then something reignites their curiosity about God. Maybe they've achieved some financial or professional success and ask, “Is that all there is?” Or after the death of a parent, they start to wonder about their own mortality. Or their children ask about God, awakening questions that have lain dormant within themselves for years. “Who is God, Mommy?"
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Thus begins a tentative journey back to their faith-though it may not be the same faith they knew as children. Perhaps a new tradition speaks more clearly to them. Perhaps they return to their original religion but in a different, and more committed, way than when they were young.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: So I firmly stepped onto the path of disbelief. This journey continued for a few months until a conversation with a mutual friend of Brad. Jacque (she pronounced it “Jackie”) came from a small town outside of Chicago and was what my friends derisively called a 'fundamentalist,' though we had scant idea of what that meant. (It meant that her faith informed her life.)
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Jacque's response nudged me onto the path of return.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Exploration
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: A few years ago, I worked with an Off-Broadway acting company that was producing a new play about the relationship between Jesus and Judas called The Last Days of Judas Iscariot. After some meetings with the actor who would play Judas, as well as the playwright and the director, I was invited to help the cast better understand the subject material. In time they asked me to serve as 'theological consultant' for the play.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Over the course of six months, I found myself talking with the actors not simply about Jesus and Judas but also about their spiritual lives, answering questions prompted by our freewheeling discussions about the Gospels, about sin and forgiveness, and about faith. Several of the actors had toggled between one religious tradition and another, seeking something that would 'fit.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: One actor, named Yetta, who played Mary Magdalene, told me that her mother was Catholic and her father was Jewish. They decided to let her choose her own religion when she was grown. “But,” she said, “I haven't chosen yet.”
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: My time with the actors was one of not only discovering the theater but also meeting people who were traveling along a path I hadn't encountered before. They were on the path of exploration.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Given their profession, this was not surprising. A good actor often researches a new role by spending time with a person from a particular background... So the idea of “exploration” comes naturally to them. Stepping into another person's shoes for a time is not that different from entering into another religious tradition for a time.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Exploration comes naturally to Americans in particular and is a theme celebrated not only in U.S. history but in our great works of literature... Our homegrown religious writers—especially the transcendentalists Ralph Waldo Emerson and Henry David Thoreau—were inner explorers. 'Afoot and lighthearted, I take to the open road,' wrote Walt Whitman, 'Healthy, free, the world before me, / The long brown path before me leading wherever I choose.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Exploration comes naturally in American faith as well. Turned off by their childhood faith, or by the failings of organized religion, and lacking extensive religious training, many Americans searching for a religion that 'fits' embark on a quest—itself a spiritual metaphor.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The benefit of walking along the path of exploration is plain. After a serious search, you may discover a tradition ideally suited to your understanding of God, your desires for community, and even to your own personality.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Likewise, returning to your original community may give you a renewed appreciation for your 'spiritual home.' Explorers may also be more grateful for what they have found and are not as likely to take their communities for granted. The most grateful pilgrim is the one who has finished the longest journey.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Another danger is a lack of commitment. Your entire life may become one of exploration— constant sampling, spiritual grazing. And when the path becomes the goal, rather than God, people may ultimately find themselves unfulfilled, confused, lost, and maybe even a little sad.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The Path of Confusion
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This final path crosses all the other ones at various points. People on the path of confusion run hot and cold with their childhood faith— finding it relatively easy to believe in God at times, almost impossible at others. They haven't 'fallen away,' but they've not stayed connected either. They cry out to God in prayer and then wonder why there doesn't seem to be an answer. They intuit God's presence during important moments, and perhaps even during religious services, but find themselves bothered by the problems of belonging to a church, synagogue, or mosque. They may pray from time to time, particularly when in dire need, and they may go to services on key holidays. But for this group, finding God is a mystery, a worry, or a problem.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The main benefit of this path is that it often helps people to finetune their approach to their childhood faith. Unlike those who consider themselves clearly religious or clearly nonreligious, these people have not yet made up their minds, and so they are constantly refining their ideas about a religious commitment.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: But confusion can lapse into laziness. Avoiding worship services because of a particular criticism can lead to leaving organized religion entirely because it's too much work, or because it takes too much energy to belong to a group that demands, say, charity and forgiveness.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Much of my adult life, before entering the Jesuits, was spent on this path. As a boy, I was raised in a loving family with a lukewarm Catholic background. My family went to church regularly, but we didn't engage in those practices that mark very religious Catholics—saying grace at meals, speaking regularly about God, praying before going to bed, and attending Catholic schools. And in college I grew increasingly confused about God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: After Jacque's mysterious answer moved me to give God another chance, I returned to church, but in a desultory way. I wasn't sure exactly what, or who, I believed in.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: That experience helped me to step off the path of confusion and onto the path of belief, which led to the Jesuits.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: THOSE ARE THE SIX paths on which many seem to travel. What does St. Ignatius have to say to people on each of those paths about finding God? The answer is: plenty.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The way of Ignatius is an invitation to those who have always believed in God, who believe in God but not in religion, who have rejected God, who are coming back to God, who are exploring, and who are confused. Ignatius's approach meets you on your path and leads you closer to God.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Socrates was addressing students, teachers, and political leaders. Emmanuel was concerned with simple, uneducated people. What one does in a classroom is different from what one does with a ninety-five-year-old grandmother whose dogmatic views are her source of joy.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

entries.content: Day 4
How 2 Good Presentation
I - Identify: know what we're talking about.
D - Define: say what it means accurately.
E - Explain: why the topic is significant & explain its parts.
A - Apply: show how it works.
Application doesn't replace explanation.
S - Synthesize: Connect to other topics.
There are three angles for understanding God: 1. Experiential, 2. Philosophical, 3. Theological.
Experiential
Name things properly.
Discern!
Jan 26 (I.D.E.A.S)
I - Identify: know what exact topic you're talking about.
D - Define: say what the topic means accurately.
E - Explain: talk about the component parts and why it is important.
A - Apply: show how your topic plays out in real life.
S - Synthesize: connect it to other topics and to concepts outside the immediate subject.

topics.summary:
The 'Six Paths' is a framework used to understand the diverse ways people relate to God and religion. These paths are not rigid categories one chooses, but rather fluid states that reflect the trajectory of a person's life, and individuals may find themselves on several paths throughout their lifetime. The six paths are: the Path of Belief (lifelong faith, often from childhood), the Path of Independence (belief in God without organized religion), the Path of Disbelief (intellectual conclusion that God may not exist), the Path of Return (coming back to faith after drifting away), the Path of Exploration (actively sampling different religious traditions), and the Path of Confusion (an ambivalent, hot-and-cold relationship with faith).

Each path is presented as having unique benefits and potential pitfalls. For instance, the Path of Belief provides a strong anchor and community but can lead to complacency or judgment of others. The Path of Disbelief fosters deep intellectual inquiry and challenges lazy answers but may wrongly expect God's existence to be proven like a mathematical theorem. Similarly, Exploration can lead to a perfect spiritual 'fit' but risks becoming a form of 'spiritual grazing' without real commitment.

The course emphasizes the importance of understanding these distinctions, especially between similar paths like Exploration, Independence, and Confusion, which differ in their attitudes toward organized religion and communal worship. The material suggests that adolescent 'disengagement' is a common starting point for moving away from a childhood faith as one seeks an individual identity.

Ultimately, the Six Paths model serves as a diagnostic tool for spiritual self-awareness. The course posits that Ignatian spirituality offers a universal approach that 'meets you on your path,' providing guidance for anyone on any of these journeys to move closer to God. It's an invitation to discern where you are and understand that every spiritual state presents an opportunity for growth.
keyTakeaways: The Six Paths are: Belief, Independence, Disbelief, Return, Exploration, and Confusion. They describe, rather than prescribe, a person's spiritual journey.
A person's position on a path is not a simple choice but is influenced by their life's trajectory and experiences. People can move between paths or be on multiple at once.
Every path has both benefits and pitfalls. For example, Belief offers community but risks complacency, while Exploration can find a perfect 'fit' but risks a lack of commitment.

It is crucial to understand the distinctions between similar paths, particularly Independence (a conscious choice for solo faith), Exploration (a positive search among many traditions), and Confusion (an ambivalent struggle with a childhood faith).
The course presents Ignatian spirituality as a way to 'meet people on their path,' suggesting that no matter one's spiritual state, there is a way to find and draw closer to God.
studyTips: Create a 6x3 table with columns for 'Path,' 'Key Benefit(s),' and 'Key Pitfall(s).' This will help you quickly compare and contrast the core tension of each journey.

Focus on the distinctions between the paths of Independence, Exploration, and Confusion. An exam question will likely ask you to differentiate them based on their attitude towards organized religion and their level of commitment.

Use the I.D.E.A.S. framework (Identify, Define, Explain, Apply, Synthesize) to structure a one-paragraph summary for each path. For 'Apply,' use the examples given (e.g., Walter Ciszek for Belief, the actor Yetta for Exploration).

Practice applying the paths to hypothetical scenarios. For example: 'A person finds institutional religion hypocritical but prays privately and feels a connection to God. Which path are they likely on?' (Independence).

Remember the overall narrative: The Six Paths are the 'diagnosis' of different spiritual states, and Ignatian spirituality is presented as the 'method' or 'guide' for growth from any of these starting points. Connect each path back to this central idea.

connections.explanation: The Six Paths demonstrate that 'Faith' is not a monolithic concept. It can be a stable foundation (Belief), a lost object to be found (Return), something intellectually rejected (Disbelief), or a confusing mystery (Confusion).

The paths of Independence and Confusion highlight the course's distinction between personal 'Spirituality' (belief in God) and 'Religion' (organized community). They show how one can exist with a complicated or non-existent relationship to the other.

The impulse to move from one path to another, especially on the Paths of Return and Exploration, is often fueled by an innate 'Desire' for God or for meaning, a central concept in Ignatian spirituality.

A person's path can be heavily influenced by their image of God. A harsh, judgmental image might push someone toward Disbelief or Independence, while a loving image might sustain someone on the Path of Belief.

quizQuestions.question: Which path is defined by a conscious decision to separate from organized religion while still maintaining a belief in God?
choices: A) The Path of Confusion
B) The Path of Independence
C) The Path of Disbelief
D) The Path of Exploration
quizQuestions.explanation: The Path of Independence is characterized by a belief in God combined with a deliberate separation from institutional religion. Confusion involves ambivalence, Disbelief rejects God, and Exploration actively samples various traditions.

quizQuestions.question: A potential pitfall for those on the Path of Belief is a 'complacency that makes one's relationship with God stagnate.' This means they might:
choices: A) Begin to doubt the existence of God.
B) Actively search for a new religious tradition.
C) Cling to childish understandings of faith that are insufficient for adult life.
D) Demand purely intellectual proof for their beliefs.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text explicitly states that a danger for the Path of Belief is clinging to ways of understanding faith learned in childhood that may not work for an adult, leading to stagnation.

quizQuestions.question: An actor who grew up with a Catholic mother and a Jewish father decides to 'choose her own religion' but hasn't yet, trying different things to see what 'fits.' This is a classic example of:
choices: A) The Path of Return
B) The Path of Confusion
C) The Path of Exploration
D) The Path of Independence
quizQuestions.explanation: The Path of Exploration is defined by this kind of active searching and sampling of different traditions to find one that 'fits' one's personality and understanding of God.

quizQuestions.question: What is a primary distinction between the Path of Confusion and the Path of Exploration?
choices: A) Exploration involves belief in God, while Confusion does not.
B) Exploration has a more positive view of religion as something to be discovered, while Confusion is marked by disappointment with religion's failings.
C) Confusion leads to a firm decision to leave religion, while Exploration never settles.
D) Exploration is done in community, while Confusion is a solitary journey.
quizQuestions.explanation: The source material distinguishes the two by noting that Exploration maintains a positive view, seeking a fit, whereas Confusion has a more negative view, being discouraged by the perceived failures of organized religion.

quizQuestions.question: A person who challenges 'lazy answers' about suffering and has a 'religious-baloney detector' is exhibiting a key benefit of which path?
choices: A) The Path of Belief
B) The Path of Return
C) The Path of Confusion
D) The Path of Disbelief
quizQuestions.explanation: The course content specifically credits those on the Path of Disbelief with having a knack for detecting hypocrisy and challenging bland reassurances, as they have often thought deeply about these issues.

quizQuestions.question: The story of Walter Ciszek, who maintained his deep Catholic faith during years of suffering in Soviet labor camps, is used to illustrate the anchoring power of which path?
choices: A) The Path of Belief
B) The Path of Return
C) The Path of Independence
D) The Path of Confusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Ciszek's story is the primary example given for the Path of Belief, showing how a faith instilled in childhood can be a powerful anchor during immense hardship.

quizQuestions.question: A major danger of the Path of Exploration is:
choices: A) Judging people on other paths.
B) Becoming lazy and avoiding the work of community.
C) Demanding only intellectual proof of God.
D) 'Spiritual grazing' and a lack of commitment where the search becomes the goal.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text warns that for the Path of Exploration, the journey itself can become the goal, leading to constant sampling without commitment, which can ultimately be unfulfilling.

quizQuestions.question: A person begins to question their childhood faith after achieving career success and asking, 'Is that all there is?' This existential question often marks the beginning of which path?
choices: A) The Path of Disbelief
B) The Path of Independence
C) The Path of Return
D) The Path of Belief
quizQuestions.explanation: The Path of Return is often initiated by life events (like achieving success, a death in the family, or a child's questions) that reignite a dormant curiosity about God and spirituality.

quizQuestions.question: Someone who prays only in dire need, attends services on major holidays, but is generally bothered by the problems of organized religion is best described as being on:
choices: A) The Path of Confusion
B) The Path of Independence
C) The Path of Exploration
D) The Path of Disbelief
quizQuestions.explanation: This hot-and-cold relationship—intuiting God's presence at times but being bothered by the church and lacking consistent connection—is the defining characteristic of the Path of Confusion.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course, the way of St. Ignatius is significant in relation to the Six Paths because it:
choices: A) Proves that the Path of Belief is the only correct one.
B) Provides a method to meet people on any path and lead them closer to God.
C) Was designed specifically for people on the Path of Disbelief.
D) Rejects all paths except for a return to one's childhood faith.
quizQuestions.explanation: The material explicitly states that the Ignatian approach 'meets you on your path,' serving as a universal invitation for people on all six paths to find God.

topics.title: Sacred

glossaryTerms.definition: Fascinans: Part of the experience of the sacred that is 'attractive'.
Mysterium: Part of the experience of the sacred that is 'beyond our imagination'.
The quotidian refers to the daily and is the antonym of the sacred.
Tremendum: Part of the experience of the sacred that is 'terrifying' and 'beyond control'.

mysterium tremendum et fascinans: A concept of the sacred described as a mystery ('mysterium': beyond our imagination, always full), that is both terrifying ('tremendum': out of our control) and fascinating ('fascinans': attractive).
When I am before something greater than myself and beyond my comprehension, that's what he calls the experience of the numinous.
quotidian: daily movements of life.
According to theologian Rudolf Otto, the experience of the sacred is the experience of the Holy or the numinous.

What I'm talking about here, everybody, is that feeling of, or that sense of grandeur that we have, that feeling of being part of something more, or that there is indeed something more. This, everyone, let's call this the experience of the sacred.

So what then is the sacred? A preliminary definition. It's hard to really come up with a clear dictionary definition. But a good place to start is this: that the Sacred is an experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning.

entries.content: Before I dive in, I really want to talk to you about adobo and why adobo specifically my Lola's adobo.

The adobo I grew up with... is an adobo that's a lot darker... My Lola serves this adobo with garlic rice and the most perfect sunny side up... That was my first glimpse of heaven. It's having my lola's adobo when I was seven years old.

Every time I have this adobo, it really does feel like an out of body experience for me. It's damn, damn good. I'm sharing this to you because this is going to be the key analogy of this entire module. My Lola's adobo.
The first is that I eat my Lola's unbelievably delicious adobo. And as I told you, it's an out of body experience. I'm still so mind blown by it even today.
All of this really as a quick summary of our four main concepts for this sacred, faith, spirituality and religion.

Here's the best reason why I love the beach... somehow whenever I stand on the shore like this, I really feel small... a better way to put it is that I get a sense that there's something far greater than me, that there's a greater scheme of things.

But of course you can feel it on a mountain too. So see, whether it's a mountain or a beach, when you're really right smack in the middle of nature and you're enveloped by its grandeur, you feel small and in some sense you have a healthy sense of self forgetfulness.

I think one of the most anxious experiences in our life is when we're so self conscious... But I think that's why people go to mountains and beaches... Because in some sense they just forget it all and they become absorbed with where they are.

For some people it's places that make them feel like they're part of a grander scheme of things. But for others it could be people. For others it could be moments. So see, whether in places or people or experiences, have we ever had those moments where we feel that life is just so much more than what we perceive?

What I'm talking about here, everybody, is that feeling of, or that sense of grandeur that we have, that feeling of being part of something more, or that there is indeed something more. This, everyone, let's call this the experience of the sacred.
It might be helpful if we examine its opposite, which is... not the word mundane, not the word profane. The way I want to approach this is to talk about the word quotidian.

The word quotidian, right? It comes from the Latin word quotidianus... And cotidianus means daily. So when we think of the quotidian, we think of daily movements of our lives, right? The conclusion is where life hammers us. It's our daily, ordinary, non flashy attempts at surviving and thriving.
I'm not saying that being part of the quotidian is a negative experience. It's not. Most of our lives are lived in the quotidian. We get life done in the daily.

But there is a possibility of getting stuck in the quotidian. You can get stuck in the Covidian in what I would describe as a washing machine existence... That life just becomes this endless meaningless cycle of just surviving.

The good news is that you don't have to get stuck... there are basic things that you can do in each day to make sure that you don't get caught up in the hamster rhythm... And you know the most basic way to do it? It's to simply ask the question, why?

I think there's a lot of negative baggage with those words. When you say profane, it's something that sort of insults the sacred. When you see mundane, you're talking about a boring version of the quotidian... So I think the word quotidian is the one that feels more neutral.

So what then is the sacred? A preliminary definition. It's hard to really come up with a clear dictionary definition. But a good place to start is this: that the Sacred is an experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning.
The sacred is this thing that opens you out to something greater than yourself. And that life is not just about going with the motions of our temporal existence.
The secret opens you up in a way, surprises you, kind of leaves you emotional at times, makes you feel moved... I think that's the feeling that you get when you really encounter a mystery.
What's the experience of the sacred like? I think it's akin to the experience of falling in love.

There's something about falling in love that has an arresting quality... It captures your attention so that you focus on this person whether you like it or not. So see the tension? I like it, but this makes me uncomfortable. That is what the experience of the sacred is like.

As the German Lutheran theologian Rudolph Otto would put it, when he talks about the experience of the sacred... he talks about this experience of being before the sacred's arresting quality as the numinous.
When I am before something greater than myself and beyond my comprehension, that's what he calls the experience of the numinous.
And he described this experience in his famous Latin description that the numinous is mysterium tremendum et fascinans.
That's why it's also tremendum. It's terrifying. It's out of your control.

Especially when you're someone who's so... very proud of your singleness... To fall in love with somebody can be such an uncomfortable experience. It somehow messes with your emotional wiring. And all of these fears will come out... What if I get rejected?

Every relationship is a risk. Guess what people, including a married man... A vow is not something like, I'm going to choose you today. And you can guarantee I'm going to choose you every time. It's choosing somebody each day. And so the risk is, what if I don't get chosen?
It's scary. It's terrifying. To be in front of the sacred is to be in such a vulnerable position that you realize you don't have full control.

And yet somehow it's fascinans. Fascinans brings about the English word as fascinating. When you're fascinated by something, somehow it pulls you towards itself, it's attractive. I don't want it, but I also want it.

Mysterium tremendum et fascinans. That... is what we get whenever we encounter, in some sense, God. And what I've come to observe throughout the years is that you don't necessarily have to be a religious person to encounter God in this manner.

topics.summary:
In Theology 11, the 'sacred' is understood not as a specific object or place, but as a profound experience that disrupts our daily routine and opens us to life's deeper meaning. This experience gives us a sense of being part of something far greater than ourselves, a 'grander scheme of things.' It stands in contrast to the 'quotidian,' which refers to the normal, everyday, and non-flashy aspects of our lives. While most of life is lived in the quotidian, encounters with the sacred prevent us from getting stuck in a monotonous rhythm, reminding us that life is more than what we typically perceive.

Theologian Rudolf Otto describes this experience of the sacred as 'numinous,' a powerful encounter with the Holy that is both awe-inspiring and overwhelming. He famously defined this encounter with the Latin phrase 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans.' This means the sacred is a mystery ('mysterium') that is beyond our full comprehension. It is also terrifying ('tremendum') because it's out of our control and makes us feel vulnerable. Yet, paradoxically, it is also fascinating ('fascinans'), attracting and pulling us towards it.

This dual nature of being both terrifying and attractive is a key characteristic of the sacred. An excellent analogy is the experience of falling in love: it can be uncomfortable and mess with your life, yet it's also deeply compelling and desirable. The sacred has an 'arresting quality' that captures our attention, whether it's found in the grandeur of nature, the complexity of human relationships, or even in ordinary, lived-in events like sharing a special family meal. By prompting us to ask 'why?' even about mundane things, we can uncover these sacred dimensions in our daily existence.
keyTakeaways: The sacred is an experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning and a sense of something greater than yourself. It contrasts with the 'quotidian,' the ordinary, daily aspects of life.
Rudolf Otto's key concept describes the sacred as 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans': a mystery that is both terrifying (tremendum) and fascinating/attractive (fascinans).
The experience of the sacred is paradoxical; it is simultaneously uncomfortable and thrilling, terrifying and attractive. The analogy of 'falling in love' perfectly captures this tension.
The sacred has an 'arresting quality' that interrupts the routine of daily life, making you feel small yet part of a grander scheme.

Encounters with the sacred are not limited to special places like churches or mountains; they can be found in people, moments, and even ordinary objects and experiences like a family recipe ('Lola's adobo').
studyTips: Master the central paradox: 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans'. Be prepared to explain this concept using the course's main analogy of 'falling in love'—how it's both terrifying and attractive. This is a likely essay or short-answer question.
Create flashcards for the key vocabulary: Sacred, Quotidian, Numinous, Mysterium, Tremendum, and Fascinans. For each term, write the definition and a simple example from the course.

Practice applying the concept by identifying your own 'Lola's adobo'. Think of a personal experience (a song, a place, a food, a person) that feels sacred to you and analyze it using the terms 'mysterium', 'tremendum', and 'fascinans'. This will help you answer application-based questions.

Focus on the distinction between 'quotidian' and other opposites like 'profane' or 'mundane'. Remember that 'quotidian' is used because it's a neutral term for the everyday, whereas 'profane' implies insult and 'mundane' implies boredom.

Connect the abstract ideas to the concrete examples given. Be able to explain how standing on a beach, falling in love, and eating a special adobo all illustrate the core definition and characteristics of the sacred.

connections.explanation: The encounter with the sacred is presented as the invitation to faith. This powerful, personal experience of something greater can be the starting point for developing a life of faith.

Wonder is the disposition required to perceive the sacred. To experience wonder is to be open and attentive to the deeper meanings that can be found even in the quotidian, which can lead to an encounter with the sacred.

The sacred is defined as a 'mysterium,' linking it directly to this concept. A problem can be solved, but a mystery (like the sacred) is something we can only enter into and experience, never fully comprehend or control.

The personal encounter with the sacred is a core component of spirituality. Religion often provides the framework, rituals, and community to help individuals interpret, cultivate, and respond to these sacred experiences.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, what is the 'quotidian'?
choices: A) The daily, ordinary, and non-flashy movements of life.
B) Anything that is boring and unfulfilling.
C) The feeling of being part of something greater than oneself.
D) An experience that is both terrifying and fascinating.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is A. The course defines 'quotidian' as the daily and ordinary, contrasting it with the extraordinary experience of the sacred. It is specifically noted as being a neutral term, not necessarily negative like 'boring' (B).

quizQuestions.question: Rudolf Otto's phrase 'mysterium tremendum et fascinans' describes the experience of the numinous. What does 'tremendum' refer to?
choices: A) The attractive and compelling quality of the experience.
B) The incomprehensible and mysterious nature of the experience.
C) The terrifying and overwhelming aspect that is beyond our control.
D) The feeling of being part of a grander scheme.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. 'Tremendum' refers to the terrifying, awesome, and uncontrollable aspect of the sacred that makes one feel vulnerable. 'Fascinans' is the attractive quality (A), and 'mysterium' is the incomprehensible nature (B).

quizQuestions.question: The course uses 'falling in love' as a key analogy for the experience of the sacred. Why is this analogy effective?
choices: A) Because both are common, everyday experiences for everyone.
B) Because both are purely positive and comfortable experiences.
C) Because it captures the paradoxical nature of being both uncomfortable/terrifying ('tremendum') and attractive/compelling ('fascinans').
D) Because both experiences can be fully understood and controlled with enough effort.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The analogy highlights the core tension in the sacred experience: it has an 'arresting quality' that can be uncomfortable and scary, yet it is also deeply attractive and pulls you in, much like the paradox of 'tremendum et fascinans'.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best defines the 'experience of the sacred' as presented in the course?
choices: A) A logical conclusion that God exists.
B) An experience that opens you to life's deeper meaning and a sense of something greater than yourself.
C) A disciplined daily practice of prayer and meditation.
D) A moment of pure happiness and comfort.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The core lessons repeatedly define the sacred as an experience that gives a 'feel of grander things,' opens you to deeper meaning, and makes you feel part of a grander scheme.

quizQuestions.question: Why does the instructor prefer the term 'quotidian' over 'profane' to contrast with the sacred?
choices: A) Because 'quotidian' is a more academic and complex word.
B) Because 'profane' is an outdated term from Latin.
C) Because 'quotidian' is a neutral term for 'daily', while 'profane' carries negative baggage of insulting the sacred.
D) Because the 'quotidian' and the 'profane' mean the exact same thing.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The 'DISTINCTIONS' section explicitly states that 'profane' has negative baggage, suggesting an insult to the sacred, while 'quotidian' is a more neutral term for the everyday.

quizQuestions.question: The feeling of being 'small' and experiencing 'self-forgetfulness' while standing on a beach or a mountain is an example of:
choices: A) A purely quotidian moment.
B) An initial encounter with the sacred.
C) A religious doctrine.
D) An experience of the 'fascinans' alone.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The course begins with this example to illustrate how nature's grandeur can provoke a sense of something greater than oneself, which is the gateway to the experience of the sacred.

quizQuestions.question: The 'arresting quality' of the sacred means that it:
choices: A) Can be legally prosecuted.
B) Is always a pleasant and welcome interruption.
C) Captures your attention and bothers your usual way of going about life.
D) Makes you want to be alone and isolated.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The course notes state that the sacred has an 'arresting quality' that 'captures your attention' and 'bothers your usual way of going about life,' disrupting the 'hamster rhythm' of the quotidian.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary function of the 'Lola's adobo' story in this module?
choices: A) To provide a recipe for students to try at home.
B) To argue that food is the only path to the sacred.
C) To serve as a key analogy for how the sacred can be found in ordinary, personal, and lived-in experiences.
D) To explain the economic importance of family traditions.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The text explicitly says, 'this is going to be the key analogy of this entire module,' demonstrating how a seemingly ordinary thing (a family dish) can be the site of an 'out of body experience'—an encounter with the sacred.

quizQuestions.question: The part of the sacred experience that is described as being 'beyond our imagination' and always full is the:
choices: A) Mysterium
B) Tremendum
C) Fascinans
D) Numinous
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is A. The definitions provided clearly state that 'Mysterium' is the part of the sacred experience that is 'beyond our imagination'. The numinous (D) is the overall experience, which includes mysterium, tremendum, and fascinans.

quizQuestions.question: A student feels an overwhelming mix of fear and attraction when contemplating the vastness of the universe. This dual feeling is a perfect example of:
choices: A) The quotidian life.
B) Tremendum and fascinans.
C) A misunderstanding of theology.
D) Mysterium only.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The fear of the vast, uncontrollable universe is the 'tremendum,' while the attraction and pull towards understanding it is the 'fascinans.' This captures the central paradox of the sacred experience.

Paul Tillich's analysis of faith is 'the state of being ultimately concerned.'
So faith. What's faith? It's the acceptance of the sacred. Personally and ultimately.

entries.content: There was something about that adobo that really inspired me to learn how to cook. I know how to cook and that's really so I believe in preserving my Lola's cooking because of this experience of her adobo. So I decided to learn how to cook like her.
The experience of something greater than yourself... After that initial experience... Now comes an invitation. An invitation to accept this experience as a part of your own life.
So faith. What's faith? It's the acceptance of the sacred. Personally and ultimately.

It's possible that your experience of the Sacred can just become a passing experience... But faith is saying, this is my experience. And there's something I want to do to make this experience very much a part of my life.
But take note of this, that the Sacred invites us to faith. ... Faith is not an imposition, but an invitation. You really cannot force faith on a person because faith is something that you choose.
The more you impose faith, the less attractive it becomes.

See, this is what a lot of people miss, that faith is not an object we have, as it is a conviction we uphold. It's interesting because whenever we talk about faith, especially in Christian theology, they often think about faith as just this entire deposit of teachings.
What you're going to see here, that faith isn't so much that list of things you say yes to, but it is a choice that you make to make the sacred a part of your life.
Look at what James Martin says in the reading.
While you already have the basics. Soil, seeds, water, you have to cultivate and nurture.

What religion does for you is that it gives you the materials to cultivate your faith. It gives you teachings, it gives you practices, it gives you certain ways of living, but you have to cultivate that yourself.

The way my faith looks like may have similarities to your faith, but it's going to be very different. Our faith can have similarities... but ultimately they are different. And I can't impose my way of relating to God to... anyone else.
Allow me to offer this idea coming from St. Thomas Aquinas.
For Thomas Aquinas he says that when it comes to the existence of God, the existence of God is not self evident.
What that means is that we cannot obviously without any controversy, say that there is a God... God's existence, that's controversial.
No one here, I think would reasonably deny the existence of air. God's not like the air we breathe. It's not like it's controversial that there's air, but God's existence, that's controversial.
Maybe that's what faith is about: it enables us to grow out of these images, especially if they tend to be distorted.
It is this very inexplicability, moreover, that may explain the origin of religious consciousness and the emergence of its principal vehicle, which is faith.

entries.content: Others sometimes envy people who walk along the path of belief. "If only I had faith like you!” one friend often tells me. While I understand her sentiment, that perspective makes faith seem like something you have rather than have to work at keeping.

entries.content: In his book A Friendship Like No Other, the Jesuit spiritual writer William A. Barry invites adults to relate to God in an adult way. Yet many believers cease their religious education as children, and expect it to carry them through adulthood. People in this group often find that they need to reeducate themselves to understand their faith in a mature way.

entries.content: In the medieval period, for example, influential figures such as Blessed Angela of Foligno (1248-1309), St Mechthild of Magdeburg (c. 1210-c. 1285) and Blessed Margaret Ebner (1291-1351) reported periods of feeling abandoned by God.

topics.summary:
This topic explores faith not as a static object to be possessed or a set of doctrines to which one must agree, but as a dynamic, personal, and chosen response to an invitation from the Sacred. Faith is presented as a conviction we uphold rather than an object we have. It begins with an experience of something greater than oneself, leading to an invitation to integrate this experience into one's life. Crucially, this is an invitation, not an imposition, meaning faith cannot be forced; it is a choice. Because God's existence is not self-evident or universally provable (like the air we breathe), faith becomes the primary way individuals relate to and make sense of the divine.

Furthermore, the course distinguishes between a childish faith and a mature, 'adult' faith. Many people cease their religious education in childhood, yet expect this rudimentary understanding to sustain them through adult life's complexities. The material argues for the necessity of cultivating an adult faith, much like one cultivates a garden—it requires work, patience, and nourishment to grow. This involves re-educating oneself and relating to God in new, more mature ways as one ages, similar to how an adult child's relationship with a parent evolves.

A central and perhaps surprising argument is that doubt is not the opposite of faith but an integral part of it. Theologian Paul Tillich is cited, insisting that 'existential doubt' is part of faith's very structure. Creative doubt keeps beliefs from becoming 'dead dogmas' and allows an individual to make inherited beliefs their own. This concept is powerfully illustrated through the 'dark night of the soul' or 'experiential atheism'—profound experiences of God's absence felt by figures like St. John of the Cross, Mother Teresa, and even Jesus on the cross. These periods of 'godforsakenness' are not seen as a failure of faith, but as a testament to its depth and the courage it requires.
keyTakeaways: Faith is a personal, chosen response to an invitation from the Sacred, not an object you possess or a belief system that is forced upon you.
A distinction is made between 'belief' (assent to dogma) and 'faith' (a personal commitment and conviction).
An 'adult faith' is necessary for an adult life and must be actively cultivated and matured, unlike a childish faith which is often static.
Doubt is not the opposite of faith; 'creative' or 'existential' doubt is a vital component that keeps faith alive, challenges it, and helps it mature.
Experiences of God's absence ('the dark night of the soul' or 'experiential atheism') are a profound part of the faith journey for some deeply committed believers, like Mother Teresa.
studyTips: Focus on the key distinctions: 'Faith vs. Belief', 'Adult vs. Childish Faith', and Tillich's types of doubt. Create a simple chart to compare and contrast these pairs.

Master the core metaphors. Be prepared to explain what the 'faith as a garden' metaphor implies (cultivation, work, patience) and what the 'faith is not a gas station' metaphor rejects (a quick, passive fix).

Connect key figures to concepts. Associate Mother Teresa, St. John of the Cross, and Padre Pio with the 'dark night of the soul' and experiential atheism. Associate Tillich with the idea of doubt being integral to faith.
Prepare to discuss the role of doubt. An likely exam question will ask you to explain why doubt can be a *positive* element in faith, using concepts like 'creative doubt' and avoiding 'dead dogmas'.

Synthesize the different definitions of faith provided: 'acceptance of the sacred,' 'state of being ultimately concerned' (Tillich), and 'a conviction we uphold.' Think about how they complement each other to form a cohesive view.

connections.explanation: The study of faith directly introduces 'experiential atheism' as seen in the 'dark night of the soul' of mystics like Mother Teresa, connecting the experience of God's absence to the deepest levels of faith, not to a rejection of God.

God's existence is presented as controversial and not self-evident, aligning it with the concept of a 'mystery' to be entered into through faith, rather than a 'problem' to be solved with absolute proof.

The need to move from a 'childish faith' to an 'adult faith' often involves confronting and dismantling distorted or overly simplistic images of God that cannot withstand the complexities and sufferings of adult life.

Faith begins with an 'experience of something greater' that sparks an interest or longing. Even in the 'darkness,' Mother Teresa's letters reveal a profound 'thirsting' and 'longing' for God, showing how desire is a driving force within faith.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, what is the primary distinction between 'faith' and 'belief'?
choices: A) Faith is a personal commitment and conviction, while belief can be mere intellectual assent to dogma.
B) Faith is for adults, while belief is for children.
C) Faith is based on doubt, while belief is based on certainty.
D) Faith is a gift from God, while belief is a human creation.
quizQuestions.explanation: The materials state that 'belief is not faith' and can be 'assent to dogma without... commitment,' whereas faith is a choice and a 'conviction we uphold.' This highlights the difference between personal commitment and simple agreement.

quizQuestions.question: The metaphor of faith being 'like a garden' primarily suggests that faith:
choices: A) Is something you are born with, like a natural landscape.
B) Can die easily and should be avoided.
C) Requires active cultivation, patience, and nourishment to grow.
D) Provides a full supply of everything you need automatically.
quizQuestions.explanation: The metaphor emphasizes that faith is not passive. Like a garden, it requires 'patience, persistence, and even work' to cultivate and nourish it, contrasting with the idea that faith is something you just 'have'.

quizQuestions.question: How do the experiences of Mother Teresa and St. John of the Cross challenge a simplistic view of faith?
choices: A) They prove that anyone who doubts for a long time is secretly an atheist.
B) They show that profound experiences of God's absence, or 'darkness,' can be part of a deep and committed faith life.
C) They suggest that only famous saints are allowed to experience doubt.
D) Their experiences show that faith eventually leads to a state of constant spiritual happiness.
quizQuestions.explanation: Their long periods of 'godforsakenness' or the 'dark night of the soul' demonstrate that faith is not a guarantee of feeling God's presence. Instead, it can involve persevering in commitment even through profound spiritual desolation.

quizQuestions.question: According to Paul Tillich, why is doubt an essential part of faith?
choices: A) Because all beliefs are probably false and should be doubted.
B) Because doubt is a sin that faith must constantly fight against.
C) Because he only considers skeptical doubt, which rejects all certainty.
D) Because existential doubt is part of faith's structure and prevents beliefs from becoming 'dead dogmas'.
quizQuestions.explanation: Tillich argues that doubt, specifically 'existential doubt,' is part of the 'very structure of faith' and 'is overcome... by courage.' Creative doubt keeps beliefs 'alive and vigorous' by challenging them.

quizQuestions.question: The course states that 'faith is not an imposition, but an invitation.' What does this imply?
choices: A) Faith is a universal requirement for all people.
B) You cannot force someone to have genuine faith; it must be freely chosen.
C) God only invites certain people to have faith.
D) An invitation to faith can be declined, but doing so is a moral failure.
quizQuestions.explanation: This statement highlights the voluntary nature of faith. As the notes say, 'You really cannot force faith on a person because faith is something that you choose.' It is a personal response to an invitation from the Sacred.

quizQuestions.question: What is meant by the need for an 'adult faith'?
choices: A) Religious education should end in adulthood.
B) A faith based on a child's understanding is often insufficient to address adult life's complexities and questions.
C) Only adults can have true faith.
D) Adult faith means rejecting all previous religious instruction.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course argues that just as a third-grader's math is insufficient for an adult, a child's religious education is often not enough to sustain a person in adulthood, necessitating a more mature, developed faith.

quizQuestions.question: Paul Tillich defines faith as:
choices: A) The acceptance of the sacred as personally and ultimately valuable.
B) The state of being ultimately concerned.
C) A conviction we uphold.
D) The experience of a 'dark night of the soul.'
quizQuestions.explanation: The text explicitly attributes this definition to Tillich: 'Paul Tillich's analysis of faith is 'the state of being ultimately concerned.'' While the other options relate to faith, this specific definition is Tillich's.

quizQuestions.question: Why, according to the materials, can we not say 'without any controversy, that there is a God'?
choices: A) Because no one has ever truly believed in God.
B) Because God's existence, unlike the air we breathe, is not self-evident and is a subject of debate.
C) Because science has disproven God's existence.
D) Because faith in God is an imposition by the church.
quizQuestions.explanation: The lesson explicitly contrasts God's existence with the existence of air, noting that the latter is not controversial while the former is. This non-self-evident nature of God's existence is what makes faith a necessary and chosen response.

quizQuestions.question: Dostoevsky's personal grappling with faith, where his 'hosanna' passed through a 'furnace of doubt,' serves as an example of:
choices: A) A weak faith that ultimately failed.
B) A faith that is shallow and untested.
C) The idea that creative doubt can play a role in making one's beliefs alive and vital.
D) The 'death of God theology' popular in the 1960s.
quizQuestions.explanation: Dostoevsky's experience exemplifies the principle that grappling with doubt is not a sign of failure but can be a process that strengthens and deepens faith, making it more robust and personal.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'experiential atheism' as used in the course?
choices: A) The intellectual rejection of God's existence based on philosophical arguments.
B) A political movement to remove religion from public life.
C) The profound, felt experience of God's absence or 'godforsakenness' by a believer.
D) A temporary phase of doubt that all teenagers go through.
quizQuestions.explanation: The material defines this term in relation to mystics who believe in God but undergo intense, prolonged periods where they cannot feel God's presence, as if God were absent. It's an experiential state, not an intellectual one.

topics.summary:
This topic explores the intricate relationship between spirituality and religion, arguing they are not opposing forces but complementary aspects of a mature faith. Spirituality personalizes religion, making it a lived experience, while religion grounds spirituality, providing it with wisdom, structure, and community. The core purpose of this synthesis is not intellectual knowledge but a profound personal transformation known as 'metanoia'—a change of heart or 'becoming'. This journey of becoming is primarily fueled by contemplative prayer and a disciplined 'attention' to God's presence, moving beyond simple requests to a deep, relational awareness.

While acknowledging the modern trend of being 'Spiritual But Not Religious' (SBNR), the material cautions against its potential pitfalls. An isolated spirituality, sometimes called 'Sheilaism,' risks becoming a self-centered and potentially distorted faith, lacking the corrective wisdom and support of a community. For example, a person might adopt a skewed view of Christianity focused on prosperity and abandon it when faced with suffering—a reality that a religious community would have prepared them for.

Ultimately, the course argues that a healthy spiritual life requires both the personal inward journey (spirituality) and the communal, historical framework (religion). Religion without spirituality can become a dry, lifeless set of rules, which Jesus warned against. Conversely, spirituality without religion can lead to a self-serving complacency divorced from a wider tradition. The goal is communion with God and transformation of the self, which is best achieved when personal spirituality and communal religion work hand in hand.
keyTakeaways: Spirituality and Religion are not contrasts; they are supplements. Spirituality personalizes religion, and religion grounds spirituality.
The ultimate goal of the spiritual journey is not what you know, but who you become—a transformative change of heart called 'metanoia'.
This 'becoming' is primarily achieved through contemplative prayer and 'attention', which fosters a deep, relational awareness of God beyond simple petitions.
A key danger of modern spirituality is 'Spiritual But Not Religious' (SBNR) or 'Sheilaism,' which can lead to a self-centered, shallow faith that lacks the corrective wisdom of a community.
Religion without spirituality becomes dry dogma, while spirituality without religion can become selfish complacency. A mature faith integrates both.
studyTips: Create a two-column chart for 'Spirituality' and 'Religion'. List the strengths of each on its own, its weaknesses on its own (e.g., 'dry dogma' for Religion, 'self-centered' for Spirituality), and then a third section on how they strengthen each other.

Make flashcards for key terms and their associated concepts/people: Metanoia (change of heart), Attention (Simone Weil, highest form of prayer), SBNR, Sheilaism (Robert Bellah, individualistic faith), and Dark Night of the Soul.

The exam will likely ask you to apply concepts. Review the examples provided (Quaker meeting, cooking adobo, the person seeking wealth from Christ) and be able to explain exactly what principle each one illustrates.

Focus on the argument, not just the definitions. Be able to clearly articulate *why* the course material critiques the 'Spiritual But Not Religious' position, using the concept of community wisdom as your main evidence.
Trace the central argument's flow: The goal is 'becoming' (metanoia), the method is 'contemplative prayer/attention', and the necessary context is the partnership of 'spirituality and religion'.

connections.explanation: This topic is defined in direct relation to Religion. The course argues they are not in conflict but are essential supplements for a mature faith.
The 'becoming' that results from contemplative prayer leads to 'openness to ever-evolving revelation,' connecting personal spiritual practice to God's self-disclosure.
The text refines the idea of faith, stating it's not *just* between an individual and God. This connects directly to the critique of SBNR, which lacks the communal dimension of faith.

An individualistic spirituality like 'Sheilaism' risks creating a god in one's own image. A religious tradition provides a communal and historical corrective to these personal, distorted images of God.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is the ideal relationship between spirituality and religion?
choices: A) They are contrasting forces, and one must choose spirituality over the confines of religion.

B) It can become a self-centered complacency divorced from community wisdom.
C) It leads to atheism and a rejection of God.
D) It is too focused on rituals like daily Mass.

choices: A) The ideal synthesis of spirituality and religion.
B) A faith that is too centered on the church hierarchy.
C) An enriching interaction with another religious tradition.
D) An individualistic, privatized faith, or being 'Spiritual But Not Religious'.
quizQuestions.explanation: 'Sheilaism' describes a faith that is 'Just my own little voice,' representing the SBNR phenomenon where faith becomes entirely individualized, lacking communal input.

quizQuestions.question: Simone Weil’s assertion that pure 'attention' is the highest form of prayer is used to illustrate:
choices: A) The importance of petition and asking God for things.
B) A disciplined spiritual practice that moves beyond petition to a simple awareness of God's presence.
C) The burden of freedom described by Dostoevsky.
D) The danger of a faith becoming a self-centered complacency.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text connects 'attention' to a growth in contemplative prayer that moves us 'beyond petition to a relationship' and a 'growing awareness of God’s presence'.

quizQuestions.question: How does the example of the person expecting financial success from following Christ illustrate a key theme?
choices: A) It shows that being religious guarantees financial success.
B) It highlights the danger of an individualistic faith that lacks the community's wisdom about suffering.
C) It proves that contemplative prayer leads to material wealth.
D) It demonstrates the superiority of Catholicism over other Christian denominations.
quizQuestions.explanation: This example shows how a community would have corrected the 'skewed view' that following Christ leads to financial success, thus preventing the person from dropping their faith when faced with hardship. It shows the weakness of spirituality without religion.

quizQuestions.question: The course material argues that freedom is a 'burden' (Dostoevsky) because:
choices: A) Freedom from religion allows for greater spiritual expression.
B) It comes with the responsibility to constantly re-evaluate the foundations of one's life.
C) True freedom can only be found by abandoning all spiritual pursuits.
D) It is an illusion and humans are not truly free.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text links Dostoevsky's insight directly to the 'responsibility of continually reevaluating the very foundations of one's life,' stating that to flee this is to flee true humanity.

quizQuestions.question: What is SBNR?
choices: A) An acronym for a Jesuit spiritual practice.
B) A term for a type of contemplative prayer.
C) An acronym for 'Spiritual But Not Religious,' describing a modern approach to faith.
D) A sociological term for the decline of church attendance.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text defines SBNR as an acronym for 'Spiritual but Not Religious' and discusses it as a common way people describe their individualized faith.

quizQuestions.question: The analogy of learning to cook adobo is used to illustrate that:
choices: A) Spirituality is about finding a recipe that works for you and never changing it.
B) An interest must be put into practice to become a meaningful part of one's life.
C) Cooking is the highest form of prayer.
D) Faith is a matter of personal taste.
quizQuestions.explanation: The analogy contrasts 'wanting to cook' with 'actually cooking,' showing that a desire must become a daily, lived practice to be real. This parallels the need for spiritual practices.

quizQuestions.question: The primary objective of the spiritual life, which will 'initiate the next phase of the kingdom on earth,' is described as:
choices: A) Accumulating knowledge about different religions.
B) Gaining followers for one's own 'little voice'.
C) Perfecting enlightened policies and sophisticated instrumentalities.
D) What one will 'become' through a process of 'metanoia'.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text states that the objective of first importance is 'this metanoia, this what-they-shall-have-become,' emphasizing personal transformation over knowledge or external policies.

glossaryTerms.definition: Isaac Hecker was a nineteenth-century convert to Catholicism who became a priest and founded the American religious order known as the Paulists. He may have summed it up best. Religion, said Hecker, helps you to 'connect and correct.' You are invited into a community to connect with one another and with a tradition. At the same time, you are corrected when you need to be.
Theology is the study ('logos') of God ('Theos').
Apologetics is the rational defence of Christian doctrine, a word rooted in 'apologia'.
Along with several other second-century Christian thinkers, Justin wrote a number of apologias - 'defences' rather than 'apologies' in the usual meaning of the word.

entries.content: You're going to notice here that I'm now going to be more explicitly religious in content.
I am also somebody who's very religious, but not in an in-your-face distance or the way I just tell people that. Church is very much an important part of my life.
A lot of my weekends are also spent doing miniscule work. So one of the main things I do is that on Sundays, yes, I am a preacher.

I'm part of a community in Alabang called the Feast. The Feast is still a Catholic community founded by Bo Sanchez, but then it's also a Catholic community that has many different branches all around the city and all around the world.

My particular piece is in Alabang, in a little place called East Alabang Northeast. That's our Instagram. If you go to our Instagram, you'll also notice that there are little reels that we make coming from the Sunday preaching.
I'm also someone who's active in my neighborhood parish. I am the one man choir in one of our Sunday masses. I play the guitar and then sing and people sing along with me.

It's my neighborhood parish. I've grown up there. I've met some of my longtime childhood friends there. I first fell in love there... meeting my neighbors through the parish. This parish consists really just a few blocks away from my house. So in a way it also serves like our village's community center.
When I tell you about religion or when I talk to you about matters of faith, I'm also coming from that context of living it out myself.

But of course I can't learn how to cook on my own. I must learn from the community of cooks, be it my mom, house helps, etc. In order for me to become a better cook... I really cannot be a good cook on my own, a lot of these techniques I learned from others.
What is said of poetry may, of course, be said as well of religion.

entries.content: Apart from its role in mitigating violence and lawlessness, religion, properly understood, has always addressed something more than ethics or social harmony.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: In its very assumption of another order of reality, its principal function has been the mediation of mystery—a function whose object, by definition, is not accessible to ordinary means of human understanding or resolution.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: The extensive relationship between religion and the arts in music, ritual, dance and the like clearly finds its origins in this shared grounding.
entries.sourceFile: R1 - John Savant - BACK TO WONDER-1 (1).pdf

entries.content: Religious traditions, unfortunately, have a tendency to straight-jacket mystery by listing it as one or another item of belief.

entries.content: This is the crabbed, joyless, and ungenerous religiosity that Jesus spoke against: spiritual blindness.

entries.content: Maybe they find church services meaningless, offensive, dull, or all three. Maybe they've been hurt by a church. Maybe they've been insulted (or abused) by a priest, pastor, rabbi, minister, or imam. Or they feel offended by certain dogmas of organized religion. Or they find religious leaders hypocritical. Or maybe they're just bored.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Believe me, I've heard plenty of homilies that have put me to sleep, sometimes literally.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: As the Catholic priest and sociologist Andrew Greeley once wrote, sometimes the question is not why so many Catholics leave the church—it's why they stay.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Catholics may be turned off by the church's teachings on a particular moral question, or its stance on a political question, or by the scandal of clergy sex abuse. Consequently, while they still believe in God, they no longer consider themselves part of the church.
