entries.content: But, as one friend said after the sex abuse crisis, “I didn't fall away from the church. It fell away from me."
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: As the Trappist monk Thomas Merton wrote in The Seven Storey Mountain, “The first and most elementary test of one's call to the religious life—whether as a Jesuit, Franciscan, Cistercian or Carthusian—is the willingness to accept life in a community in which everybody is more or less imperfect.” That holds for any religious organization.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: This is not to excuse all the problems, imperfections, and even sinfulness of religious organizations. Rather, it is a realistic admission that as long as we're human, we will be imperfect.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: When I was a novice, one of my spiritual directors quoted the Scottish philosopher John Macmurray, who contrasted 'real religion' and 'illusory religion.' The maxim of 'illusory religion' is as follows: 'Fear not; trust in God and He will see that none of the things you fear will happen to you.' 'Real religion,' said Macmurray, has a different maxim: 'Fear not; the things you are afraid of are quite likely to happen to you, but they are nothing to be afraid of.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The thinking goes like this: being religious means abiding by the arcane rules and hidebound dogmas, and being the tool of an oppressive institution that doesn't allow you to think for yourself (which would have surprised many thinking believers, like St. Thomas Aquinas, Moses Maimonides, Dorothy Day, and Reinhold Niebuhr). Religion is narrow-minded and prejudicial—so goes the thinking- stifling the growth of the human spirit (which would have surprised St. Francis of Assisi, Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, St. Teresa of Ávila, Rumi, and the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.).
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Or worse, as several contemporary authors contend, religion is the most despicable of social evils, responsible for all the wars and conflicts around the world.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Sadly, religion is responsible for many ills in the modern world and evils throughout history: among them, the persecution of Jews, endless wars of religion, the Inquisition, not to mention the religious intolerance and zealotry that leads to terrorism.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: You can add to this list smaller things: your judgmental neighbor who loudly tells you how often he helps out at church, your holier-than-thou relative who trumpets how often she reads the Bible, or that annoying guy at work who keeps telling you that belief in Jesus is sure to bring you amazing financial success.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: There is a human and sinful side to religion since religions are human organization, and therefore prone to sin. And, frankly, people within religious organizations know this better than those outside of them.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Still, I would stack up against the negatives the positive aspects: traditions of love, forgiveness, and charity as well as the more tangible outgrowths of thousands of faith-based organizations that care for the poor, like Catholic Charities or the vast network of Catholic hospitals and schools that care for poor and immigrant populations.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Think too of generous men and women like St. Francis of Assisi, St. Teresa of Ávila, St. Catherine of Siena, Mother Teresa, and the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Speaking of Dr. King, you might add abolition, women's suffrage, and the civil rights movements, all of which were founded on explicitly religious principles.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Add to that list the billions of believers who have found in their own religious traditions not only comfort but also a moral voice urging them to live selfless lives and to challenge the status quo.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: And Jesus of Nazareth. Remember him? Though he often challenged the religious conventions of his day, he was a deeply religious man. (This is something of an understatement.)
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: On balance, I think religion comes out on top.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: And when I think about the maleficent effects of religion, I remember the English novelist Evelyn Waugh, a dazzling writer who was by many accounts a nasty person. (He once wrote to his wife, 'I know you lead a dull life now. . . But that is no reason to make your letters as dull as your life. . . . Please grasp that.') One of Waugh's friends, Nancy Mitford, once expressed astonishment that he could be so mean-spirited and a Christian. 'You can't imagine,' said Waugh, 'how much worse I should be if I were not religious.'
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Because this would mean that you, alone, are relating to God. And that means there's no one to suggest when you might be off track. We all tend to think we're correct about most things, and spiritual matters are no exception.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Not belonging to a religious community means less of a chance of being challenged by a tradition of belief and experience. It also means less chance to see that you are misguided, seeing only part of the picture or even that you are wrong.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Despite our best efforts to be spiritual, we make mistakes. And when we do, it's helpful to have the wisdom of a religious tradition.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Religion can provide a check to my tendency to think that I am the center of the universe, that I have all the answers, that I know better than anyone about God, and that God speaks most clearly through me.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: By the same token, religious institutions need themselves to be called to account. And here the prophets among us, who are able to see the failures, weaknesses, and plain old sinfulness of institutional religion, play a critical role.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Like individuals who are never challenged, religious communities can often get things tragically wrong, convinced that they are doing 'God's will.' (Think of the Salem witch trials, among other examples.) They might even encourage us to become complacent in our judgments. Unreflective religion can sometimes incite people to make even worse mistakes than they would on their own.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Thus, those prophetic voices calling their communities to continual self-critique are always difficult for the institution to hear, but nonetheless necessary.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Ignatius, for example, exercised a prophetic role by asking Jesuits not to seek high clerical office in the church-like that of bishop, archbishop, or cardinal. In fact, Jesuits make a promise not to 'ambition' for high office even within their own order. In this way, Ignatius not only tried to prevent careerism among the Jesuits, but also spoke a word of prophecy to the clerical culture rampant in the Catholic Church of his time.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Isaac Hecker was a nineteenth-century convert to Catholicism who became a priest and founded the American religious order known as the Paulists. He may have summed it up best. Religion, said Hecker, helps you to 'connect and correct.' You are invited into a community to connect with one another and with a tradition. At the same time, you are corrected when you need to be.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: And you may be called to correct your own community—though a special kind of discernment and humility is required in those cases.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Religion can lead people to do terrible things. At its best, though, religion modifies our natural tendency to believe that we have all the answers. So despite what many detractors say, and despite the arrogance that sometimes infects religious groups, religion at its best introduces humility into your life.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Religion also reflects the social dimension of human nature. Human beings naturally desire to be with one another, and that desire extends to worship. It's natural to want to worship together, to gather with other people who share your desire for God, and to work with others to fulfill the dreams of your community.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Finally, religion means that your understanding of God and the spiritual life can more easily transcend your individual understanding and imagination. Do you imagine God as a judge? That's fine-if it helps you become a more moral and loving person. But a religious tradition can enrich your spiritual imagination in ways that you might not be able to discover by yourself.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: The image of the God who surprises and the God who waits for surprises came to me from three Jesuit priests and the religious imagination of a Catholic writer. In other words, that idea was given to me by religion.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Reflection on Mill's point should give rise to a toleration that acknowledges that most religions seek to reflect ultimate value. The question then becomes which religion (which set of symbols) reflects ultimate value most adequately.

In Theology, the focus is specific; it is not a study of everything.

topics.summary:
This topic presents a balanced and nuanced view of religion, acknowledging its dual capacity for profound good and immense harm. The lesson begins by confronting the undeniable negative history of religion, including wars, persecution, and intolerance, attributing these to its nature as an imperfect human institution prone to sin. However, this is contrasted with the immense positive contributions, such as traditions of love and charity, the establishment of hospitals and schools, and the inspiration for major social justice movements like civil rights. The text argues that religious communities provide essential social connection and a framework for communal worship.

Beyond its social and ethical dimensions, the core function of religion is defined as the 'mediation of mystery'—addressing a reality that transcends ordinary human understanding. It serves as a vital check on individual ego and the tendency to believe one has all the answers. By participating in a religious tradition, an individual's personal, and often limited, spiritual imagination is enriched and expanded. The wisdom of a tradition provides guidance, especially when an individual makes mistakes or develops a distorted view of God.

The central practical takeaway is summarized by Isaac Hecker's principle of 'connect and correct.' Religion connects you to a community of fellow believers and a deep historical tradition. Simultaneously, this community and tradition serve to correct your personal blind spots, challenge your self-centeredness, and foster humility. Even prophetic voices that critique the institution from within are seen as a necessary, albeit difficult, part of this corrective function, ensuring the religion's continual call to self-improvement and faithfulness to its core mission.
keyTakeaways: Religion has a dual nature: it is responsible for historical evils (wars, intolerance) but has also inspired immense good (charity, love, social justice movements).
The principal function of religion is not merely ethics, but the 'mediation of mystery'—addressing a reality beyond ordinary human understanding.
The 'Connect and Correct' principle (Isaac Hecker) is key: Religion connects individuals to a community and tradition, and corrects personal arrogance and blind spots.

Because religions are human organizations, they have a 'sinful side' and are imperfect. Recognizing this is a realistic admission, not an excuse, and necessitates prophetic voices for internal critique.
Organized religion provides an essential check on individual ego, enriches one's spiritual imagination beyond personal limitations, and fulfills the human desire for communal worship.
studyTips: Create a two-column chart for 'Positives/Functions of Religion' vs. 'Negatives/Critiques of Religion'. This will help you organize the central tension of the topic and prepare for compare/contrast questions.

Focus on memorizing the 2-3 key phrases and their authors: 'Connect and correct' (Isaac Hecker), 'real vs. illusory religion' (John Macmurray), and the definition of 'apologetics'. Exam questions often link concepts to specific figures.

Prepare for application questions. Think about how you would apply the 'connect and correct' principle or the idea of the 'sinful side' of religion to a contemporary issue, like a church scandal or a faith-based humanitarian effort.

Master the course's argument against a purely individualistic spirituality. Be ready to explain *why* the text argues that a religious community is valuable for challenging your own biases and preventing spiritual arrogance.

Pay attention to the examples used, like Evelyn Waugh or Martin Luther King Jr. Be prepared to explain what concept each example illustrates (e.g., Waugh illustrating religion's power to modify behavior, even if imperfectly).

connections.explanation: This topic on 'Religion' provides the communal and traditional framework for the personal journey of 'Spirituality'. It argues that religion can ground, challenge, and enrich one's spirituality, preventing it from becoming self-centered.

The lesson's realistic admission that religion is 'imperfect' and has a 'sinful side' directly counters the trap of 'Perfectionism'. It teaches that value and grace can be found within flawed human institutions.

The text argues that a key function of religion is to expand your spiritual imagination beyond your own limited ideas. This communal tradition can 'correct' personal, and potentially 'Distorted Images of God', by offering a richer, time-tested understanding.

While 'Faith' is a personal act, 'Religion' is the communal context where that faith is expressed, nurtured, and lived out. This topic shows how religious communities are the tangible, social expression of a shared faith.

quizQuestions.question: According to Isaac Hecker, the principle of 'connect and correct' means that religion...
choices: A) connects you to a community and tradition, which in turn corrects your personal blind spots and arrogance.
B) connects your actions to consequences and corrects your behavior through fear of punishment.
C) connects you directly to God, correcting the need for any community or institution.
D) corrects historical errors by connecting with modern scientific thought.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer reflects Hecker's idea that religion's value lies in providing both community ('connect') and a mechanism for humility and self-correction ('correct') through that community and its tradition.

quizQuestions.question: The course content argues that the principal function of religion is primarily to:
choices: A) ensure social harmony and provide a system of ethics.
B) mediate mystery and address an order of reality beyond ordinary understanding.
C) provide comfort and explain away the difficult parts of life.
D) organize people into powerful political and social blocs.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text explicitly states that while religion does influence ethics and social harmony, its 'principal function has been the mediation of mystery—a function whose object... is not accessible to ordinary means of human understanding.'

quizQuestions.question: How does the text frame the 'human and sinful side' of religious organizations?
choices: A) As an unforgivable flaw that invalidates religion entirely.
B) As a secret that religious insiders try to hide from the public.
C) As a realistic admission that, like all human institutions, they are imperfect and prone to sin.
D) As a problem that was solved after the Reformation and the Inquisition.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text presents this as a 'realistic admission,' stating that 'as long as we're human, we will be imperfect,' and that people inside religious organizations often know this better than anyone.

quizQuestions.question: The anecdote about the novelist Evelyn Waugh, who was a nasty person despite being a Christian, illustrates which point?
choices: A) That religion is ineffective and has no impact on a person's character.
B) That all religious people are hypocrites.
C) That at its best, religion modifies and restrains our worst tendencies, even if it doesn't perfect us.
D) That artistic talent and religious belief are incompatible.
quizQuestions.explanation: Waugh's comment, 'You can't imagine how much worse I should be if I were not religious,' perfectly illustrates the idea that religion can serve to modify negative tendencies, introducing a check on behavior even in an imperfect person.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would be an example of the 'prophetic role' described in the text?
choices: A) A church leader who predicts future stock market trends to raise funds.
B) A member who publicly criticizes their community's complacency or sinfulness, calling it back to its core values.
C) A believer who insists that their personal interpretation of scripture is the only correct one.
D) A historian who writes a book praising all past actions of a religious institution.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text describes prophetic voices as those who 'are able to see the failures, weaknesses, and plain old sinfulness of institutional religion' and call their communities to 'continual self-critique.' This is a critical, corrective role played from within.

quizQuestions.question: What is the definition of 'apologetics'?
choices: A) The practice of apologizing for the historical sins of a religion.
B) The study of God through silent prayer and meditation.
C) The rational defense of Christian doctrine.
D) A genre of writing that expresses sorrow for personal failings.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text defines apologetics as 'the rational defence of Christian doctrine, a word rooted in 'apologia',' which means 'defence'.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is a primary risk of a spirituality that is disconnected from a religious community and tradition?
choices: A) It is less likely to be joyful and emotionally fulfilling.
B) It is more likely to become self-centered and arrogant.
C) It cannot lead to a genuine belief in God.
D) It will inevitably lead to financial ruin.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text argues that religion provides a 'check to my tendency to think that I am the center of the universe' and that not belonging to a community means 'less chance to see that you are misguided.'

quizQuestions.question: John Macmurray's distinction between 'real' and 'illusory' religion suggests that 'real religion'...
choices: A) promises that God will prevent bad things from happening to you.
B) provides the courage to face life's difficulties, acknowledging they are likely to happen.
C) focuses solely on intellectual belief, not on real-world events.
D) is only found in ancient, unreformed religious traditions.
quizQuestions.explanation: The quote defines 'real religion' with the maxim: 'Fear not; the things you are afraid of are quite likely to happen to you, but they are nothing to be afraid of.' This emphasizes courage in the face of reality, not escape from it.

quizQuestions.question: How does the text characterize the relationship of Jesus of Nazareth to the religion of his day?
choices: A) He was an outsider who sought to completely abolish all organized religion.
B) He was a deeply religious man who often challenged the religious conventions and spiritual blindness of his time.
C) He was a political revolutionary who used religion as a tool for social upheaval.
D) He was a strict traditionalist who upheld every existing religious rule without question.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text states, 'Though he often challenged the religious conventions of his day, he was a deeply religious man,' and notes that he spoke against 'crabbed, joyless, and ungenerous religiosity.'

quizQuestions.question: The text argues that religion helps expand one's spiritual imagination. How does it do this?
choices: A) By forcing everyone to imagine God in exactly the same way.
B) By providing a single, perfect image of God that answers all questions.
C) By introducing an individual to a rich tradition that offers more ways of understanding God than one could invent alone.
D) By proving that imagination is more important than rational thought in theology.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text states, 'a religious tradition can enrich your spiritual imagination in ways that you might not be able to discover by yourself,' suggesting it provides a broader palette of metaphors and concepts for God beyond an individual's limited perspective (e.g., God is only a judge).

topics.title: Perfectionism

glossaryTerms.definition: In the context of perfectionism, 'Chaos' can be defined as the randomness and possible scenarios you didn't expect.
A form of ego defined by overinflating the benefit.
A form of ego defined by focusing on yourself.
In the context of perfectionism, 'Order' can be defined as relying on pre-thought solutions and overthinking.
Flawless; when nothing goes wrong and nothing needs to be added.
Perfectionism is excessive control over the processes of one's life, situations, difficulties, and paradoxes.

This feeling of God as a harsh parent is often accompanied with a lot of shame. The whole idea of 'I didn't measure up'.
Perfectionism is about control. An image of God can become a form of perfectionism if it's used to control God or reality.
The main danger for this group, however, is a perfectionism that sets up any organized religion for failure.

entries.content: Not long ago, a friend stopped attending his family's church. My friend is an intelligent and compassionate man who believes in God and whose parents had deep roots in Episcopalianism. But he believed his local church was too aligned with the affluent. So he decided to search for a community that recognized the place of the poor in the world. After he left his church, he toyed with the idea of joining the local Catholic church, which he noticed many of the poor attended on Sundays. But my friend disagreed with their prohibition on ordaining women. So he rejected Catholicism. Next he experimented with Buddhism, but he found it impossible to reconcile his belief in a personal God, and his devotion to Jesus Christ, with the Buddhist worldview. Finally, he ended up at the local Unitarian church, which initially seemed to suit him. He appreciated their broad-minded Christian- based spirituality and commitment to social justice, as well as their welcome of people who feel unwelcome in other churches. But he eventually ran into a problem: the Unitarians didn't espouse a clear enough belief system for my friend. In the end, he decided to belong to no church. Now he stays at home on Sundays.

topics.summary:
In Theology 11, perfectionism is presented not as a positive drive for excellence, but as a significant spiritual and psychological obstacle. It is defined as an excessive need for control over one's life and a demand that reality conform to one's personal expectations. This mindset is rooted in ego—a focus on oneself—and often stems from or reinforces a distorted image of God as a harsh parent, leading to feelings of shame for not 'measuring up.' The core danger of perfectionism is its static nature; it seeks a flawless state where nothing can go wrong, which is fundamentally opposed to the messiness of real life.

The course outlines several key harms stemming from this mindset. First, perfectionism disables us from seeing life as it truly is, sheltering us from the very experiences necessary for development. Second, by positing an ideal state, it renders growth—which happens through imperfection and challenge—unnecessary or impossible. Finally, this rigid worldview makes individuals more prone to harming themselves and others when reality inevitably fails to meet their impossible standards. This is why the course states that perfectionism always has victims, be it oneself or those around them.

The theological alternative to perfectionism is embracing the 'Good Life' as a dynamic state of growth rather than a static state of perfection. This involves a shift in focus from perfect results to a meaningful process—the 'chase for perfectioning.' Growth is found not in 'Order' (overthinking and pre-planned solutions) but in 'Chaos' (the unexpected, the failures, the things that don't go our way). As illustrated by the metaphor of an imperfectly shaped puzzle piece, our flaws are what allow us to connect to a bigger picture. The ultimate takeaway is to embrace imperfection and value presence and process over flawless performance, encapsulated by the maxim, 'Do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good.'
keyTakeaways: Perfectionism is a form of excessive control that stifles personal and spiritual growth by preventing one from engaging with life as it truly is.
The 'Good Life' is not a static state of perfection but a dynamic process of growth ('perfectioning'), which often occurs through chaos and failure.
Perfectionism is inherently harmful, making individuals prone to hurting themselves and others because it resists reality and has no room for grace or error.
In a religious context, perfectionism is a major pitfall that can lead one to reject all traditions because none are flawless, resulting in spiritual isolation.
The antidote to perfectionism is to embrace imperfection, focus on the process rather than the result, and understand that our flaws are often what connect us to others and to a larger reality.
studyTips: Focus on the core contrast: Be able to clearly explain the difference between the 'static state of perfection' (the goal of perfectionism) and the 'dynamic state of growth' (the goal of the 'Good Life'). Use the puzzle piece metaphor in your explanation.

Memorize the three main harms of perfectionism: 1) Disables us from seeing life as it is, 2) Renders growth impossible/unnecessary, and 3) Makes us prone to harm. Expect an exam question that asks you to list or apply these concepts.

Master the 'Order vs. Chaos' framework. Understand that for this topic, 'Order' means rigid control and overthinking, while 'Chaos' represents the unexpected events where true growth happens. This is a key conceptual model for the course's view on perfectionism.

Analyze the main example: The story of the friend searching for a church is the primary case study. Be prepared to write a short answer explaining how his perfectionistic standards led him from one church to another and ultimately to isolation. This demonstrates the practical danger of perfectionism in spirituality.

Connect definitions to consequences. Don't just memorize that perfectionism is 'excessive control.' Understand *how* that desire for control leads to the negative outcomes discussed, such as stifled growth and harm to others.

connections.explanation: Perfectionism is often linked to a distorted image of God as a harsh parent or demanding taskmaster. This view fosters a belief that we must be flawless to earn divine love, creating shame and hindering a relationship based on grace.

Perfectionism is a significant pitfall for spiritual seekers, especially on paths like 'independence.' The demand for a flawless community or belief system can lead to rejecting all available options, resulting in isolation rather than spiritual belonging.

A perfectionist treats life's challenges as 'problems' to be solved with perfect, controlled solutions. Theology encourages viewing them as 'mysteries' to be lived and experienced, which requires accepting ambiguity and imperfection—the opposite of a perfectionist mindset.

Perfectionism seeks certainty and absolute control, which are antithetical to faith. Faith requires trust and vulnerability in the face of the unknown, while perfectionism tries to eliminate the unknown altogether.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, what is the primary definition of perfectionism?
choices: A) The pursuit of excellence and high standards in one's spiritual life.
B) A form of ego involving excessive control over the processes and outcomes of life.
C) The belief that God demands flawless behavior from believers.
D) A personality trait characterized by being highly organized and detail-oriented.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The course explicitly defines perfectionism as 'excessive control' and links it to a form of ego that wants reality to adjust to it, distinguishing it from a healthy pursuit of excellence.

quizQuestions.question: The course argues that the 'Good Life' is a 'dynamic state of growth.' This stands in direct contrast to the perfectionist's goal, which is a...
choices: A) state of continuous improvement.
B) state of spiritual enlightenment.
C) static state of perfection.
D) state of communal harmony.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The materials create a direct opposition between the 'dynamic state of growth' (process-oriented) and the 'static state of perfection' (a flawless, unchanging outcome) that the perfectionist seeks.

quizQuestions.question: In the story of the friend who leaves his church, his inability to find a spiritual home illustrates which danger of perfectionism?
choices: A) It makes one prone to harming other people's beliefs.
B) It sets up any organized religion for failure because none is perfect.
C) It leads to an over-reliance on a personal God.
D) It creates a distorted image of God as a harsh parent.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The friend examines and rejects multiple churches (Episcopalian, Catholic, Buddhist, Unitarian) for their specific imperfections, demonstrating the core lesson that perfectionism's demand for a flawless institution leads to rejecting all real-world options.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three primary harms of perfectionism identified in the core lessons?
choices: A) It disables us from seeing life as it truly is.
B) It renders growth unnecessary or stifles it.
C) It makes us more prone to harm ourselves and others.
D) It encourages a reliance on luck rather than effort.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is D. The course explicitly lists A, B, and C as the key 'disablers' of perfectionism. While it mentions you cannot run on luck, it does not state that perfectionism *encourages* a reliance on it.

quizQuestions.question: The metaphor of a person as an imperfectly-shaped puzzle piece suggests that...
choices: A) individuals are inherently flawed and incomplete.
B) a perfect life is like a completed puzzle.
C) our imperfections are what allow us to connect to a bigger picture.
D) one must find the right pieces to solve the puzzle of life.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The metaphor's point is that a perfectly smooth, flawless piece cannot connect to anything. The 'imperfect' shape—the gaps and extensions—is what allows it to interlock with others, suggesting our flaws and unique qualities are essential for community and connection.

quizQuestions.question: In the context of perfectionism, 'Chaos' is best understood as:
choices: A) A state of complete disorder and moral decay.
B) The randomness and unexpected scenarios where growth can occur.
C) The emotional turmoil caused by failing to meet a goal.
D) A theological concept representing evil or the absence of God.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The glossary and core lessons define 'Chaos' not as a negative state to be avoided, but as the unpredictable parts of life ('the randomness and possible scenarios you didn't expect') that challenge the perfectionist's control and are therefore opportunities for growth.

quizQuestions.question: How does the course suggest one should judge 'mediocrity'?
choices: A) By the final result, regardless of the effort.
B) By the intention behind the action.
C) By comparing the outcome to a perfect standard.
D) By the process, not the result.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is D. The course explicitly states, 'Mediocrity is judged in the process, not in the results.' An example given is trying badly (a mediocre process) but getting a good result anyway.

quizQuestions.question: The feeling of God as a 'harsh parent' who makes you feel 'I didn't measure up' is identified as a common companion to:
choices: A) Faith
B) Wonder
C) Perfectionism
D) Revelation
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The core lessons directly link the spiritual danger of perfectionism to viewing God as a harsh judge and the subsequent shame of feeling one has failed to meet impossible standards.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course, growth happens primarily when:
choices: A) You have a clear plan and full control over the situation.
B) You rely on pre-thought solutions to avoid mistakes.
C) Things don't go your way and you encounter chaos.
D) You achieve a flawless and perfect result.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is C. The materials repeatedly emphasize that 'Growth happens when things don't go your way' and 'Growth happens in chaos.' This directly counters the perfectionist's desire for control and predictable outcomes.

quizQuestions.question: The quote 'Do not let the perfect be the enemy of the good' serves as a concise summary for which central idea?
choices: A) Striving for perfection eventually leads to good results.
B) Rejecting imperfect but good options in search of a flawless one can lead to having nothing.
C) Good intentions are more important than perfect actions.
D) The concept of 'perfect' is an illusion and only the 'good' is real.
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is B. This maxim warns against the perfectionist trap illustrated by the friend seeking a church: by rejecting good-but-imperfect options in favor of an unattainable perfect one, you can end up worse off. It is an exhortation to accept goodness, even when it's not flawless.

topics.title: Distorted Images of God

glossaryTerms.definition: A distorted image of God as a "Distant Puppeteer," related to Watchmaker theory, where God has left the world to operate on its own. People who hold this view believe God left the world alone, which leads to COMPLACENCY.
A distorted image of God as an "Enabling Friend" who "feeds your delulu" (delusions), emphasizes COMFORT, permits everything, and ultimately leads to a "SUGAR CRASH".
A distorted image of God as a "Harsh Parent" who demands conformity, expects perfection, and whose primary tool is SHAME.
To worship symbols is to worship the pointers to God rather than God; and to worship something other than God is, by definition, idolatry.

God becomes what one writer called a 'pocket-size God,' small enough to put in your pocket when God doesn't suit you (for example, when the Scriptures say things that you would rather not hear) and take out of your pocket only when convenient.
A distorted image of God as a "Strict Enforcer" who is focused on compliance and only loves those who follow the rules.

The distant puppeteer. Now this image is of God simply being this distant controller of the universe, someone who just controls the universe in some grand sense... But that's it. God really doesn't care about human affairs.
The enabling friend... This is a God who yeah, expects me to be good in some general sense, but he doesn't set any real standards for me to follow.
The harsh parent... this is a God who sets a high bar of moral and spiritual perfection for you to be, but does not help.
God is the strict enforcer. This is a God who makes rules for you to follow and is eager to punish you if you don't follow them.

entries.content: Our first contact with anybody always gives impressions. And think of that word impression, right? A synonym for that is the word image.
Quite often in any relationship, we always operate on some image we have of a person.

Maybe as you get closer to that person, you have a high resolution image of that person... Quite often when you meet a person for the first time, you still have a very blurry image. And that blurry image is often formed by our biases too.
According to Dr. Chris Pritchett in his recent lecture, Healing Our Images of God and Ourselves...

He says our cognitive beings are acceptable through reason, but our inner images are formed through experiences in deeper and more powerful ways from the messages we have received primarily in childhood, but even throughout our lives as well.

If I asked one of you... 'can you define for me what a father is?' ...he will not give me a dictionary definition... Most likely, when I say define what a father is, who will you think about? Your own father.
We do not start with concepts because you do not know someone conceptually. Experience tells us that we get to know someone first through our images or our impressions of them.
There is a difference between our concept of God, the concepts that maybe we believe or we were told to believe, and what our real images of God are.
According to Pritchett, who you think God is, is based on your images, not your concepts. Who God really is for you, is based on your images of God, not your concept.

You have been told that God is a loving father... But then what if your own experience of fatherhood isn't good? ... How can you reconcile loving father, that whole concept, with your actual image of a father that comes from your experience? There's a disconnect between our concepts and our images.

One of the things that people, you know, that scripture always says is God is always watching. Some find that to be a comforting thought, but there are people who find that a little bit creepy. ... It's that God is surveilling every little thing I do.
Allow me to show you now four distorted images of God.
God is the strict enforcer. This is a God who makes rules for you to follow and is eager to punish you if you don't follow them.
This image [The Strict Enforcer] emphasizes compliance.
The moment a relationship becomes all about the rules, it then becomes a burden.
The harsh parent... this is a God who sets a high bar of moral and spiritual perfection for you to be, but does not help.
This one [The Harsh Parent] emphasizes conformity... the harsh parent has this picture perfect performative image of you that you have to fit.

These are two distorted images that negate the human person. They're negative images because... they limit your humanity, they shrink it. You're going to see in these next two images that these are images that now are positive... distorted images... they over emphasize the human being.
The enabling friend... This is a God who yeah, expects me to be good in some general sense, but he doesn't set any real standards for me to follow.
The enabling Friend emphasizes comfort, that you do not have to follow any real moral or spiritual standard because no matter what you do, God is going to permit everything that you do.
How is it a problem when you think that love means permitting everything that a person does?

Be careful with the word enabled, right? Because it might seem like you're simply respecting a person's decisions, but if you're allowing them to take a path that's not good, it could actually lead to their death.

The distant puppeteer. Now this image is of God simply being this distant controller of the universe, someone who just controls the universe in some grand sense... But that's it. God really doesn't care about human affairs.

One old way of describing this is how the catechism would talk about the watchmaker theory... people used to think that when God created the world, he left it alone for us to figure it out on our own.
It emphasizes complacency... this is a God who doesn't care about me. And so I don't really have to care about living a good life or being a moral person.
The strict enforcer emphasizes compliance. The harsh parent emphasizes conformity. The enabling friend, comfort. The distant puppeteer emphasizes complacency.
Maybe you know it's a distorted image if it elicits those kinds of death dealing life sucking emotions.
Why are these images distorted? I have two answers for that. The first is their lack of balance.
Here's the second reason why these images are distorted. Because they project human brokenness.
These images display the worst of humanity rather than the best that we can be.
People have used God as an instrument to inflame their egos, avoid their uncertainties and control those around them.
Be careful with how God is imaged in our minds and hearts. Because who you think is God might really just be your very strict Lola or your uncaring father, just projected as some sort of deity.
The images of God I showed you are just projections of broken people rather than the image of the living God.
In our last class we talked about God from an experiential angle, paying attention to our images of God, especially those formed in childhood.
The four distorted images of God are: the stern enforcer, the harsh parent, the enabling parent, and the distant puppeteer.
These images are distorted precisely because of their lack of balance. They emphasize something that you can truly say about God, but then they exaggerate it.
These images are also distorted because they tend to reflect our egos more than they reflect who God really is.
Having an image of God is helpful, as we are tangible beings. It becomes reductionistic if we reduce God to our images, and that image then becomes an idol—a God we create.

For example, you might cling to a childhood notion of a God who will never let anything bad happen. When tragedy strikes, since your youthful image of God is not reflected in reality, you may abandon the God of your youth. Or you may abandon God completely.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Otherwise, one remains stuck in a child-like view of God that prevents fully embracing a mature faith.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: When I was a boy, for instance, I used to think of God as the Great Problem Solver who would fix all my problems if I just prayed hard enough. Let me get an A on my social studies test. Let me do well in math. Better yet, let tomorrow be a snow day.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: As I grew older, the model of God as the Great Problem Solver collapsed-primarily because God didn't seem interested in solving all of my problems. I prayed and prayed and prayed, and all my problems still weren't solved. Why not? I wondered. Didn't God care about me? My adolescent narcissism led to some serious doubts, which led me to consider the possibility that God didn't exist.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Yet I was still stuck with a big question: if God wasn't the Great Problem Solver, the God of my youth, who was He? Or She? Or It?
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: An even greater danger for explorers is not settling on any one religious tradition because it doesn't suit them: God may become someone who is supposed to satisfy their needs.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: God becomes what one writer called a 'pocket-size God,' small enough to put in your pocket when God doesn't suit you (for example, when the Scriptures say things that you would rather not hear) and take out of your pocket only when convenient.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: So for several years God the Problem Solver was replaced by a more amorphous spiritual concept: God the Life Force, God the Other, God the Far-Away One. While these are valid images of God, I had no idea that God could be anything but those abstract ideas. And I figured that things would stay that way until I died.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: Here's an example: one of my favorite images of God is the God of Surprises, which I first encountered in the novitiate. My own idea of God at the time was limited to God the Far-Away One, so it was liberating to hear about a God who surprises, who waits for us with wonderful things. It's a playful, even fun, image of God. But I would have never come up with it on my own. It came to me from David Donovan, my spiritual director, who had read it in a book of that same title, by an English Jesuit named Gerard W. Hughes, who borrowed it from an essay by the German Jesuit Karl Rahner.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: That image was amplified when I read the conclusion of one of the great modern spiritual novels, Mariette in Ecstasy. Ron Hansen, an award-winning writer who is also an ordained Catholic deacon, penned the story of the religious experiences of a young nun in the early 1900s, loosely based on the life of St. Thérèse of Lisieux, the French Carmelite. At the end of the story, Mariette, who had left the monastery many years before, writes to her former novice director and assures her that God still communicates with her.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: God and 'the gods'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin's point here is to drive a wedge between the false, so-called 'gods' of the Roman world and the authentic God whom Christians proclaim as Lord.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin on the Roman gods: 'we not only deny that they . . . are gods, but assert that they are wicked and impious demons' (First Apology, 5).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This point is made more explicit in another (and perhaps also second-century) text, sometimes ascribed to Justin himself but now considered more likely to be the work of a Justin-inspired disciple, Pseudo-Justin.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Pseudo-Justin, this 'true and invariable Name' (On the Sole Government of God, 6) is only correctly 'applicable to the only true God' (Government, 2). Any others given the name, including those whom the pagans call gods, receive it unworthily.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Hence referring to Greco-Roman mythology, he speaks dismissively of 'those who think that they shall share the holy and perfect Name, which some have received by a vain tradition as if they were gods' (Government, 5).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: A very similar idea, although expressed slightly differently, appears in the New Testament in 1 Corinthians 8.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In the course of giving practical advice on the eating of food sacrificed to idols, Paul also distinguishes between the 'so-called gods in heaven or on earth' and the 'one God' (8.5-6).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Paul seems to oscillate between implying that these so-called gods are simple fictions ('no idol in the world really exists') and affirming that though real enough in themselves, these 'many gods and many lords' certainly do not deserve to share the divine name.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But regardless of whether they are demons or mere figments, Paul's basic point is clear: 'for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things are and through whom we exist' (1 Cor. 8.4-6).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Rather, he is anxious that the Corinthians be vigilant against lapsing, or inadvertently causing others to lapse, into idolatry - that is, into the offering of worship due to 'the Most True God' to those who are falsely so-called.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Recent converts, in particular, might be fooled into thinking that 'the gods', though falsely so-called, are actually deserving of the name: 'Since some have become so accustomed to idols until now, they still think of the food they eat as offered to an idol; and their conscience, being weak, is defiled' (8.7).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Hence, lest the practice of eating food sacrificed to idols, though in itself innocuous enough, become 'a stumbling-block to the weak', Paul advises against it. Better to go without cheap meat than to confuse others about the distinction between the misnamed 'gods' and the Most - and, for that matter, only - True one.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: As far as Pseudo-Justin is concerned, the word 'God' is not a description of something but is rather a proper name. For him, the classical gods and goddesses - Zeus, Hera, Hermes, Aphrodite and the rest - are imposters.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Now, as a description of the One worshipped and glorified by the ancient Israelites, and thus latterly the early Christians too, that is a fairly awful one.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Naturally, there is no danger whatsoever of anyone in their right mind mistaking this Poppy's metaphorical name for a literal description... Yet, as McCabe points out, and as Paul and Pseudo-Justin were all too aware, such is not the case with the Christian God.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: To think that God is simply a 'god', even if the biggest and best one of them all, is to commit idolatry.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For all of them, the worship of false 'gods' in whatever form - whether Olympian super-things, demons, fictions, or naive theological constructions - amounts to the same: idol worship.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The conceptions of God that atheists deny have, it is claimed, no bearing at all on the God in whom Christians actually believe: such 'so-called gods' are naive and idolatrous caricatures, and Christian thinkers have already made short (and rather more sophisticated) work of dismissing them.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: [I]n the sense in which atheists of this sort say God 'does not exist', that atheist has merely arrived at the theological starting-point. Theologians of the classical traditions, an Augustine, a Thomas Aquinas or a Meister Eckhart, simply agree about the disposing of idolatries, and then proceed with the proper business of doing theology. (Denys Turner)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

topics.summary:
This topic explores how our relationship with God is primarily shaped by 'images' rather than abstract 'concepts'. These images, often formed unconsciously from childhood experiences, are powerful but can become distorted. The course identifies four key distorted images: the Strict Enforcer (demanding compliance), the Harsh Parent (demanding conformity and using shame), the Enabling Friend (offering comfort without standards), and the Distant Puppeteer (leading to complacency). These images are problematic because they are unbalanced, exaggerating one of God's attributes at the expense of others. They are often projections of our own brokenness, fears, and egos, reflecting the 'worst of humanity' rather than the divine.

Fundamentally, these distorted images are a modern way of understanding the classical theological problem of idolatry. Just as early Christian thinkers like Paul and Pseudo-Justin distinguished between the 'one true God' and the 'so-called gods' of the pagan world, this topic urges us to differentiate between the authentic God and the naive, 'pocket-size' caricatures we create. To cling to these incomplete or false images—to treat God as a 'super-thing' or an instrument for our own needs—is to worship the symbol rather than the reality it points to. 

The practical takeaway is the importance of 'creative doubt' and spiritual maturity. We are called to move beyond the shadowy, simplistic images of our youth, much like in Plato's allegory of the cave. By recognizing that atheists often reject these same distorted images, we can see that true faith involves a continual process of purifying our understanding, challenging our own symbols, and remaining open to a God who is always greater than our limited human imagination.
keyTakeaways: We relate to God first through 'images' formed by personal experience, not abstract 'concepts'. A bad experience with a human father can distort the image of God as a 'loving father'.

The four primary distorted images are: the Strict Enforcer (focus on compliance), the Harsh Parent (focus on conformity), the Enabling Friend (focus on comfort), and the Distant Puppeteer (leading to complacency).
These images are 'distorted' because they lack balance, exaggerate one divine attribute, and are often projections of our own egos and brokenness rather than reflections of God.
Clinging to a distorted image of God is a modern form of the classical theological problem of idolatry—worshipping a human-made caricature instead of the true God.
Authentic faith requires 'creative doubt' and a willingness to move beyond simplistic, 'pocket-size' images toward the mystery of the true God, who is always beyond our full comprehension.
studyTips: Create a 4x4 study chart: List the four distorted images in the first column. In the next columns, write their primary emphasis (e.g., Compliance), the negative outcome (e.g., death-dealing emotions), and a real-life example for each. This will help you quickly recall and compare them.

Focus on the 'why'. An exam will likely ask not just *what* the distorted images are, but *why* they are distorted. Be prepared to explain the concepts of 'lack of balance' and 'projection of our egos'.

Master the connection between 'distorted images' and 'idolatry'. Be able to explain how Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 8 about 'so-called gods' is a classical parallel to the modern psychological concept of distorted images. This demonstrates a deeper understanding of the material.

Practice explaining the key distinctions in your own words, especially 'image vs. concept' and 'helpful symbol vs. idol'. Use the 'father' example to explain the image/concept gap and the 'pocket-size God' example to explain idolatry.

Look for 'application' style questions. The exam might present a scenario (e.g., 'Someone feels God doesn't care about their daily life...') and ask you to identify the corresponding distorted image (Distant Puppeteer). Practice applying the four categories to different beliefs.

connections.explanation: Distorted images arise when we try to reduce the 'Mystery' of God into a solvable 'Problem'. A 'pocket-size God' is an attempt to have easy, certain answers, avoiding the challenge of a God who is beyond our full understanding.

The 'Harsh Parent' image of God is a direct theological source for religious perfectionism. It creates a dynamic where one feels they must earn God's love through flawless performance, leading to shame and anxiety rather than grace.

This topic clarifies that what many atheists reject are the distorted, idolatrous images of God (like the Strict Enforcer). As the course material suggests, theologians would agree with dismissing these caricatures, seeing it as a theological starting point.

True faith is contrasted with clinging to distorted images. Faith requires moving beyond static, idolatrous images and engaging in 'creative doubt' to continually purify one's understanding and relationship with the living God.

The warning that 'to think that God is simply a 'god', even if the biggest and best one of them all, is to commit idolatry' directly connects to this theme. Distorted images treat God as a being or 'super-thing' with exaggerated human traits, rather than as Being itself.

quizQuestions.question: A person believes God loves them unconditionally and will permit any action without consequence, leading them to neglect moral or spiritual growth. This reflects which distorted image of God?
choices: A) The Harsh Parent
B) The Enabling Friend
C) The Strict Enforcer
D) The Distant Puppeteer
quizQuestions.explanation: The Enabling Friend is the image of God that emphasizes comfort and permissiveness to an extreme, suggesting no real standards need to be followed. This leads to complacency, not genuine love.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, what is the primary reason the four images (Strict Enforcer, Harsh Parent, etc.) are considered 'distorted'?
choices: A) They are not mentioned in the Bible.
B) They are entirely false with no basis in truth.
C) They lack balance by exaggerating one attribute of God.
D) They were invented by modern atheists.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core lesson is that these images are distorted 'precisely because of their lack of balance.' They take something true about God (e.g., God's justice or love) and exaggerate it to the exclusion of all other attributes.

quizQuestions.question: The theological concept of 'idolatry' is connected to distorted images of God because it involves:
choices: A) Worshipping statues made of stone.
B) Worshipping symbols or pointers to God rather than God himself.
C) Denying the existence of God entirely.
D) Following the rules of a specific religion too closely.
quizQuestions.explanation: As defined in the text, idolatry is worshipping 'the pointers to God rather than God.' Clinging to a distorted, human-made image is a form of this, as one is worshipping their own naive construction instead of the true God.

quizQuestions.question: Which distorted image is most closely associated with the 'watchmaker theory' and leads to a feeling of complacency?
choices: A) The Distant Puppeteer
B) The Enabling Friend
C) The Harsh Parent
D) The Strict Enforcer
quizQuestions.explanation: The Distant Puppeteer is the image of a God who creates the universe and then leaves it to run on its own, not caring about human affairs. This is explicitly linked to the watchmaker analogy and the resulting emotion of complacency.

quizQuestions.question: The course emphasizes that our initial understanding of God comes from images formed by experience, rather than concepts. What primary example is used to illustrate this disconnect?
choices: A) Comparing God to a character in a novel.
B) The experience of a beautiful sunset.
C) The scribbled drawing of a king.
D) One's personal experience of their own father versus the concept of a 'loving father'.
quizQuestions.explanation: The example of one's own father is used to show how a powerful, personal image (from experience) can conflict with or override a theological concept ('God is a loving father'), revealing the primacy of images in our spiritual lives.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Harsh Parent' image of God is characterized by a demand for conformity and perfection, and its primary tool is:
choices: A) Punishment
B) Shame
C) Indifference
D) Forgiveness
quizQuestions.explanation: The glossary and definitions explicitly state that the 'Harsh Parent' demands perfection and conformity, and 'whose primary tool is SHAME.' This distinguishes it from the 'Strict Enforcer,' who is more focused on rules and punishment.

quizQuestions.question: Thinkers like Paul (in 1 Corinthians 8) and Pseudo-Justin distinguish between the 'one true God' and 'so-called gods'. How does this relate to the topic of distorted images?
choices: A) It proves that all images of God are false.
B) It suggests that polytheism is the only alternative to Christianity.
C) It provides a classical theological precedent for rejecting naive, idolatrous caricatures of God.
D) It shows that ancient people were not as sophisticated as modern people.
quizQuestions.explanation: This ancient distinction is presented as a parallel to the modern task of discerning between the true God and our distorted, idolatrous images. Both involve rejecting false conceptions to pursue the authentic.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of a 'pocket-size God' refers to an image of God that is:
choices: A) All-powerful and all-knowing.
B) Convenient, non-challenging, and only brought out when it suits one's needs.
C) Formed exclusively from reading the catechism.
D) So mysterious that it cannot be understood at all.
quizQuestions.explanation: The definition states a 'pocket-size God' is one you can 'put in your pocket when God doesn't suit you... and take out... only when convenient.' It represents an idolatrous, self-serving image.

quizQuestions.question: According to theologians like Denys Turner and Étienne Borne, what is the relationship between atheism and false images of God?
choices: A) Atheism is the ultimate sin because it rejects God entirely.
B) Atheism properly criticizes false and misleading images of God, which is a task theologians also share.
C) Atheism is caused by a lack of emotional experience.
D) Atheism and theology have nothing in common.
quizQuestions.explanation: The quotes from these theologians suggest that when atheists reject a caricature of God (like the Strict Enforcer), they are 'merely at the theological starting-point.' They are doing the necessary work of 'disposing of idolatries,' which is a task shared by thoughtful believers.

quizQuestions.question: The course suggests that 'creative doubt' is a positive spiritual practice because it:
choices: A) Ultimately leads to the conclusion that God does not exist.
B) Allows you to switch religions whenever you are unhappy.
C) Serves as a check against the idolatrous worship of one's own religious symbols.
D) Confirms that your childhood images of God were correct all along.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text states that 'creative doubt... serves as a check against the idolatrous worship of one's own religious language.' It prevents us from confusing our limited symbols and images with the infinite reality of God.

topics.title: Christian "Atheism"

glossaryTerms.definition: Christian atheism can be described as a kind of spiritual emptiness or barrenness: 'I'm doing all of these things for God, but how come I don't feel God?'
Experiential atheism is that feeling of abandonment and God's forsakenness. It's when you believe in God intellectually ('up here'), but you don't experience God or see God in your experience.

glossaryTerms.term: The dark night of the soul
glossaryTerms.definition: A key concept related to a feeling of abandonment by God is 'The dark knight of the soul.'

entries.content: There are some people who can reasonably say that there is no God and live their lives as if there is no God. You have a lot of reasonable atheists.
You have people who profess to have faith but then act in an atheistic sort of manner.
A key concept related to a feeling of abandonment by God is 'The dark knight of the soul.'
Experiential atheism is that feeling of abandonment and God's forsakenness. It's when you believe in God intellectually ('up here'), but you don't experience God or see God in your experience.
And yet somehow that experience of God forsakenness didn't deter their faith. It might have even deepened their strength.
The heart of the reading by Bultmann is that Christianity is actually a form of atheism—not atheism as we understand it, but a denial of the pagan gods.
The pagan gods of Greece and Rome were often associated with things that occur in the world, like Zeus (thunder), Poseidon (the sea), and Dionysus (wine).
These pagan gods were characterized as being egotistical, temperamental, human-like, and requiring appeasement.
The ego of the pagan gods is the way that the Greeks and the Romans would try to explain or express the instability of nature.
In pagan understanding, you must appease a god with an offering to manipulate them into doing what you want. This implies the worshipper is actually more powerful than the god.
The heart of Christian atheism is this whole idea that when Christians talk about God, God is not bound by nature. God is not bound by ego. Why? Because God is not a being.
Atheists are right in saying there is no evidence of God, if 'God' is understood as just one more existing thing (a being) in the universe.
The problem with the Greek gods is that they really are just things or objects—'super-objects'—that can be controlled and manipulated. They are 'beings'.
Christian atheism can be described as a kind of spiritual emptiness or barrenness: 'I'm doing all of these things for God, but how come I don't feel God?'
Feeling God's absence can sometimes be a period of spiritual growth, or it may mean we have to widen our expectations about how God communicates with us.
Often they seek proof for God's existence, and finding none, or encountering intense suffering, they reject the theistic worldview completely.

entries.content: Likewise, sometimes the most selfless people in our world are atheists or agnostics. Some of the hardest working aid workers I met in my time working with refugees in East Africa were nonbelievers. The “secular saint” is real.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: One of my college friends practices his atheism religiously; his questions have kept me on my toes for the last thirty years. Try telling him about "God's will," and you will find yourself on the receiving end of a pointed lecture on personal responsibility.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: They coalesced when my freshman-year roommate was killed in an automobile accident during our senior year. Brad was one of my closest friends, and his death was almost too much to bear. At Brad's funeral, on a humid spring day in a wealthy suburb outside of Washington, DC, I sat in a tasteful Episcopal church, surrounded by Brad's shattered family and my grieving friends, and thought about the absurdity of believing in a God who would allow this. By the end of the service I had decided not to believe in a God who would act so cruelly. The Great Problem Solver wasn't solving problems but creating them.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: My newfound atheism was invigorating. Not only did I feel like a person with a first-rate intellect, I was proud to have rejected something that obviously had not worked. Why believe in a God who either couldn't or wouldn't prevent suffering? Atheism was not only intellectually respectable but also had some practical benefits: I now had my Sunday mornings free.
entries.sourceFile: R2 - James Martin SJ - THE SIX PATHS (1).pdf

entries.content: By the way, atheism doesn't have a perfect record either. In his book No One Sees God: The Dark Night of Atheists and Believers, the writer Michael Novak points out that while many atheist thinkers urge us to question everything, especially the record of organized religion, atheists often fail to question their own record. Think of the cruelty and bloodshed perpetrated, just in the twentieth century, by totalitarian regimes that have professed 'scientific atheism.' Stalinist Russia comes to mind.

entries.content: Picture the scene: it's festival day in a provincial Roman city in the mid-second century AD, and people have come from far and wide to witness a group of local Christians being put to death.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: A young man called Germanicus stands in the arena, not just bravely facing savage beasts but actively urging them on.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Irritated by the youth's composure, the crowd who have gathered to see him torn apart cry out: 'Down with the atheists!'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Slightly later, the venerable bishop Polycarp is brought into the arena, and is ordered - on pain of death - to himself denounce his fellow Christians in the same way.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Whereupon, we are told: 'Polycarp's brow darkened as he threw a look round the turbulent crowd of heathens in the circus; and then, indicating them with a sweep of his hand, he said with a growl and a glance to heaven: "Down with the atheists!"' (Martyrdom of Polycarp, 9).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: [Footnote 1] The translation here (slightly amended) is taken from Maxwell Staniforth (trans.), 1968, Early Christian Writings, Harmondsworth: Penguin, pp. 153-67.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: St Polycarp, whom ancient tradition assures was a disciple of St John the Evangelist, was bishop of Smyrna (modern-day Izmir on the west coast of Turkey) for much of the first half of the second century.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This gripping, eyewitness account of his and his companions' martyrdom around the year 156 is the earliest such report we have, and hence offers invaluable insights into ancient Christian life, thought and practice.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: FAITH AND UNBELIEF
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For our purposes, the word translated as 'atheists' here is our old friend, atheoi.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Given the context, its meaning here is rather more transparent than it was in Ephesians. In effect, we have the Christian Polycarp and the pagan crowd mutually denouncing each other as 'infidels'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: As far as the crowd is concerned, the Christians deny and dishonour the real Roman gods, while affirming their own false one.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: At one point, the arena crowd cry out that Polycarp is a 'destroyer of our gods, who is teaching whole multitudes to abstain from sacrificing to them or worshipping them' - Martyrdom, 12.

entries.content: But as far as Polycarp is concerned, the crowd do precisely the opposite.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: On one level, this is just an instance of a broad phenomenon noted in the Introduction, of 'atheist' being used throughout much of history as a term of abuse: it is always one's enemies who are the 'atheists' (and the feeling is often mutual).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: However, in the writings of St Justin Martyr, one of Polycarp's contemporaries, the same basic idea receives a somewhat different twist.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin had himself been brought up a pagan, but in later life, having worked his way through a succession of different philosophies - Stoicism, Pythagoreanism, Aristotelianism and Platonism - he finally settled on Christianity as the 'true Philosophy'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: [Footnote 2] This, and all other quotations from Justin's and Pseudo-Justin's (see below) writings, may be found in Alexander Roberts and James Donaldson (eds), 1996, The Ante-Nicene Fathers, Volume I: The Apostolic Fathers Justin Martyr Irenaeus, Edinburgh: T & T Clark. These and a huge number of other translated texts from the early Church Fathers can also be found at: www.newadvent.org/fathers/.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Roughly a decade after Polycarp met his fate in Smyrna, Justin was himself martyred.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In his apologias, Justin attempted to explain what and why Christians believed for his pagan contemporaries, and to correct common misunderstandings about them. The charge of 'atheism' was one of these.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin, however, novelly chooses to side with both Polycarp and the crowd, declaring in his First Apology: 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort [i.e. the Greco-Roman ones] are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God' (First Apology, 6).
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Justin then, there is at least one, albeit qualified, sense in which Christians are, and must be, atheists.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Justin is far from alone within the tradition in identifying forms of genuinely Christian 'atheism', by which I mean perfectly orthodox forms and varieties of 'atheism' within Christianity.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: These forms of 'Christian atheism' should not be confused with what one might call forms of 'Christian' atheism, such as the twentieth-century 'death of God' theologies.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: After all, it is rather the point of Christianity that its dead God has a habit of not staying so for very long.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: While the rest of this book will concern itself with the theology of what one might call atheism proper (i.e. atheism as the absence of belief in a God or gods, understood in the normal way, and hence unadorned by quotation marks), it will be a fruitful and hopefully interesting prelude to consider instead the 'atheism' of proper theology.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: We will find ourselves in good company: Paul, Justin Martyr, Augustine, Meister Eckhart, Herbert McCabe, John of the Cross and Mother Teresa will all be joining us on our adventure.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The first move for us to make here is to consider more deeply Justin's above quoted remark: 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Paul, as for Justin, Christians must therefore be atheists regarding 'the gods'. And nor is this merely an academic, highfalutinly theological point for him.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: If so, then Christians 'proclaim ourselves atheists' in a still deeper sense than Justin himself envisages.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: On this reading, Eckhart's utterance is radical enough to be fully orthodox, and a further instance of the kind of theological 'atheism' we have divined in the writings of Paul, Justin, Pseudo-Justin and McCabe.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The worship of [the] Creator is the only worship worthy of a human being... the human being is now defined, if you like, as the Creator-worshipper, the atheist with no gods to worship, no gods to petition, no gods to pray to, no gods worth praying to. (Herbert McCabe, God Still Matters)
