entries.content: The Marxist philosopher Ernst Bloch, in his rich and remarkable 1972 book Atheism in Christianity, famously observed: 'Only an atheist can be a good Christian; only a Christian can be a good atheist.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: More recently, the Slovenian intellectual Slavoj Žižek writes: 'not only is Christianity (at its core, if disavowed by its institutional practice) the only truly consistent atheism, it is also that atheists are the only true believers.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Bloch and Žižek are both atheists by our definition, at least in the 'negative' sense of being without a belief in the existence of a God or gods, and the points they are making here are largely political and ethical.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: In the theological literature on atheism, it is possible to identify two main tendencies along these kinds of line. The first is quite negative, and dismisses atheistic ideas and arguments as being, at best, obvious and platitudinous, and at worst, wholly beside the point.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The second tendency, meanwhile, takes the same basic point, but gives it a rather more positive spin. On this view, the atheist is cast as the anti-idolater par excellence, rejecting all (mis)conceptions of both God and the gods wholesale, and being, at least for the most part, right to do so.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: And if this is the case, then aren't atheists on to something very profound - and authentically Christian - after all?
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Evidence of both these two tendencies occurs in the writings of the Russian novelist Fyodor Dostoevsky.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: However, compare the following from a slightly later novel, 1872's Demons. Here the Orthodox bishop Tikhon tells the unbeliever Stavrogin: 'A complete atheist stands on the next-to-last upper step to the most complete faith.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: For Tikhon, the 'complete atheist' is one who has rejected every single false 'god', including everything and anything that takes, or tries to take, the rightful place of the true God. Such a purified position is placed higher than all 'incomplete' forms of faith.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Both tendencies arguably tell us far more about Christian theology than they do about actual atheists.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: While there may well be a certain correlation between atheism and negative theology, it is surely rather a stretch to identify the two too closely. For they, atheists sans inverted commas, are without a belief in the existence, not only of gods but of the Most True God as well.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Conclusion
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: The purpose of this opening chapter was intentionally provocative: to show the ways in which 'atheism' - although not atheism in the normal sense - sits close to the heart of classical Christian theology and spirituality.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: To a certain degree, this helps us to see some of the points of constructive contact between faith and unbelief. For example, atheists' critiques can indeed aid us in purifying our ever-faulty notions of 'the God who is not a god'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But it should also help us to guard against naive claims as to the alleged affinities between genuine atheism and authentic Christianity.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: Eventually it is understood that Emmanuel, upon whom the villagers depend so thoroughly, no longer believes certain religious doctrines that have provided for his parishioners such peace and happiness.
entries.sourceFile: Robert Baird - THE CREATIVE ROLE OF DOUBT IN RELIGION.pdf

Atheists ironically do good theology.
They reject misconceptions about God.

topics.summary:
This topic explores the provocative and paradoxical concept of "Christian 'atheism'". It's crucial to understand that this is not atheism in the modern sense (the denial of God's existence), but rather a theological stance rooted in early Christianity. The core idea, articulated by figures like St. Justin Martyr, is that Christians are 'atheists' concerning the pagan gods of Greece and Rome. These gods were understood as finite, egotistical 'beings' or 'super-objects' bound by nature, who could be manipulated through sacrifice. Early Christian theology rejected these idols, proposing instead a 'Most True God' who is not a being among other beings, but is transcendent, unbound by nature, and beyond human manipulation.

The lesson extends this idea into two further areas. First, it examines the modern philosophical perspective from thinkers like Ernst Bloch and Slavoj Žižek, who argue that "only an atheist can be a a good Christian." This suggests that someone who rightly rejects simplistic, distorted, or idolatrous images of God (like a cosmic problem-solver or a temperamental tyrant) is actually closer to true faith than someone who worships such a caricature. The atheist, in this view, becomes an 'anti-idolater', clearing the way for a more authentic understanding of God.

Finally, the topic distinguishes this theological 'atheism' from "experiential atheism." This latter concept, also known as the "dark night of the soul," describes the subjective experience of God's absence or abandonment, even while maintaining an intellectual belief. It's the feeling of spiritual emptiness or barrenness where one believes in God but cannot feel or perceive God's presence in their life. This is presented not as a failure of faith, but as a profound part of the spiritual journey for many believers, from St. John of the Cross to Mother Teresa.
keyTakeaways: Christian 'atheism' is not the denial of God, but the denial of false, pagan, or idolatrous 'gods' that are conceived as finite, manipulable 'beings'.

Early Christians like St. Polycarp and St. Justin Martyr were called 'atheists' by the Romans for refusing to worship the state gods, a label they provocatively embraced to distinguish their 'Most True God'.
A central theological argument is that God is 'not a being' in the way a person, object, or even a pagan god is a being. God is transcendent and cannot be controlled or fully comprehended.
Experiential atheism (or 'the dark night of the soul') is the feeling of God's absence despite holding an intellectual belief in God. This is distinct from theological 'atheism'.

Modern thinkers like Bloch and Žižek suggest that atheists who reject distorted images of God are performing a theologically valuable act of anti-idolatry, making them 'the only true believers' in a paradoxical sense.
studyTips: Create a comparison chart with three columns: Modern Atheism, Christian 'Atheism' (Theological), and Experiential Atheism. For each, define it, list key figures/examples (Stalin, Justin Martyr, Mother Teresa), and its core stance on God.

Focus on the key distinction: Pagan gods as 'beings' or 'super-objects' vs. the Christian God as 'not a being'. Be prepared to explain what this means using the examples of Zeus (thunder) vs. the Creator who is not bound by nature.

Memorize and understand the context of two key quotes: Justin Martyr's 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort are concerned' and Ernst Bloch's 'Only an atheist can be a good Christian'. Be ready to explain what they mean in an essay question.

An exam question might ask you to apply these concepts. For example: 'Using course concepts, analyze the experience of someone who loses their faith after a personal tragedy.' Use the example of Brad's death to explain how rejecting a 'Great Problem Solver' God is an example of rejecting a distorted image of God.
Don't confuse 'Christian atheism' (the theological concept of rejecting false gods) with 'death of God' theology. The course notes specifically distinguish these, making it a likely point of testing.

connections.explanation: This is the most direct connection. Christian 'atheism' is the practical application of the principle that God is not a being, by rejecting all gods who are conceived of as mere 'beings' or objects, like the Greco-Roman deities.

The 'atheism' discussed in this topic is essentially a rejection of distorted images of God. The atheist who says 'I don't believe in a God who is a cruel old man in the sky' is, from this perspective, rightly rejecting an idol.

This topic challenges a simple definition of faith. It shows that faith can include profound doubt and the feeling of God's absence (experiential atheism), and that true belief might involve being 'atheistic' towards false gods.

The story of Brad's death illustrates this connection. The turn to atheism came from viewing God as a 'Great Problem Solver' who failed. The theological argument of Christian 'atheism' supports moving away from seeing God as a solution to a problem and instead as a mystery.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course materials, why were early Christians like St. Polycarp called 'atheists' by the Romans?
choices: A) They secretly did not believe in any divine being.
B) They refused to worship the Roman gods and emperor.
C) They taught that God was an impersonal force of nature.
D) They were followers of a movement known as 'death of God' theology.
quizQuestions.explanation: The term 'atheoi' was used against early Christians because they refused to participate in the state-sanctioned worship of the Greco-Roman gods, which was seen as a civic and religious duty. They were not atheists in the modern sense (A) or 'death of God' theologians (D), and their God was personal, not an impersonal force (C).

quizQuestions.question: What is the central idea behind St. Justin Martyr's statement, 'we confess that we are atheists, so far as gods of this sort are concerned, but not with respect to the Most True God'?
choices: A) Christianity is a stepping stone to true, complete atheism.
B) He is admitting that Christians have no logical proof for God.
C) He is drawing a distinction between the false, finite pagan 'gods' and the one transcendent, true God.
D) He is appeasing his Roman accusers by partially agreeing with them.
quizQuestions.explanation: Justin Martyr is co-opting the charge of 'atheism' to make a theological point. He agrees they are 'atheists' towards the Roman gods ('gods of this sort'), whom he considers false idols, but they are believers in the 'Most True God' who is of a completely different nature.

quizQuestions.question: How does Bultmann and the course material characterize the pagan gods of Greece and Rome?
choices: A) As abstract philosophical principles of virtue.
B) As 'super-objects' or 'beings' bound by nature and ego that can be manipulated.
C) As historical figures who were later deified for their great deeds.
D) As manifestations of the one 'Most True God' for a different culture.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core critique is that pagan gods are just magnified, powerful 'beings' or 'objects' with human-like egos (B). They are not abstract principles (A) or different manifestations of the true God (D), but rather a fundamentally different and incorrect category of divinity.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best defines 'experiential atheism' or 'the dark night of the soul'?
choices: A) A philosophical position that God's existence cannot be proven through experience.
B) The rejection of all religious practice as meaningless.
C) The feeling of God's absence or abandonment despite intellectually believing in God.
D) The belief system of totalitarian regimes like Stalinist Russia.
quizQuestions.explanation: Experiential atheism is a spiritual state of feeling barrenness or divine absence (C), not a philosophical argument (A), a rejection of practice (B), or a political ideology (D). It's the gap between what one believes 'in their head' and what one feels or experiences.

quizQuestions.question: What is the main point of Ernst Bloch's quote, 'Only an atheist can be a good Christian; only a Christian can be a good atheist'?
choices: A) All Christians must go through a period of literal atheism to have strong faith.
B) Christianity and atheism are ultimately the same belief system.
C) A good Christian must be 'atheistic' towards false gods/idols, and a good atheist rightly rejects those same idols.
D) Political atheism and religious belief are compatible.
quizQuestions.explanation: This paradoxical statement suggests a shared purpose. A 'good atheist' rejects simplistic and false images of God, which is a task a 'good Christian' must also do (being an 'atheist' toward idols). They are both engaged in a form of anti-idolatry.

quizQuestions.question: The story of the student's friend, Brad, whose death led to a rejection of God, is used to illustrate:
choices: A) The ultimate failure of the Christian faith in the face of suffering.
B) The correct theological rejection of God as a manipulable 'Great Problem Solver'.
C) The superiority of 'scientific atheism' in explaining tragedy.
D) A classic case of the 'dark night of the soul'.
quizQuestions.explanation: Within the context of this lesson, this student's rejection is seen as a rejection of a *distorted image* of God—a God who is supposed to solve all problems. Theologically, this is a correct rejection of an idol, even if it leads to what we call atheism. It is not a 'dark night' because belief itself is jettisoned, not just felt as absent.

quizQuestions.question: The theological principle 'God is not a being' is central to Christian 'atheism' because:
choices: A) It suggests God does not actually exist.
B) It differentiates the transcendent Creator from the pagan gods, who were conceived as powerful beings or objects.
C) It proves that only atheists can be true believers.
D) It means God cannot be experienced or felt in the world.
quizQuestions.explanation: This principle is the dividing line. The 'atheism' of early Christians was precisely a rejection of gods that were 'beings'. By stating God is 'not a being', theology places God in a different category altogether, making the worship of pagan gods idolatry.

quizQuestions.question: The course materials distinguish 'Christian atheism' (like Justin Martyr's) from:
choices: A) The atheism of St. Polycarp.
B) The philosophy of Plato.
C) Twentieth-century 'death of God' theologies.
D) The concept of 'The dark night of the soul'.
quizQuestions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'These forms of ‘Christian atheism’ should not be confused with what one might call forms of ‘Christian’ atheism, such as the twentieth-century ‘death of God’ theologies.' This distinction is highlighted as important.

quizQuestions.question: In the account of Polycarp's martyrdom, when the crowd shouts 'Down with the atheists!' and Polycarp repeats the phrase back, what is the profound irony?
choices: A) Polycarp is secretly an atheist and is confessing at the last moment.
B) Both the crowd and Polycarp are referring to the Roman emperor.
C) The crowd means the Christians, but Polycarp turns the accusation back on the pagan crowd, identifying them as the true 'atheists' in relation to the 'Most True God'.
D) Polycarp is confused and does not understand the accusation.
quizQuestions.explanation: The irony lies in the double meaning. The crowd uses 'atheists' to label those who deny the Roman gods. Polycarp, with a 'glance to heaven', uses the same word to label those who deny the one true God—the pagan crowd itself. He reclaims and redefines the term.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course content, which of the following figures would be associated with 'experiential atheism' or the 'dark night of the soul'?
choices: A) Joseph Stalin
B) St. Justin Martyr
C) Ernst Bloch
D) Mother Teresa
quizQuestions.explanation: The materials mention that Mother Teresa, along with John of the Cross, experienced this profound sense of God's absence for long periods, making her a prime example of 'experiential atheism'. Stalin represents political atheism (A), Justin Martyr represents theological 'atheism' (B), and Bloch is a modern philosophical atheist (C).

topics.title: God Is Not a Being

glossaryTerms.definition: A class of supernatural beings with impressive but limited powers.
Being - ens (noun)
Efficient Cause: who or what made something. Everything has an efficient cause.
Efficient: what or who made it?
Final Cause: the purpose of a thing.
Final: what is its purpose?
Formal Cause: the arrangement or form of a thing.
Formal: how is it arranged?
Material Cause: what something is made of.
Material: what is it made of?
Disciple of Justin.
By being we mean that God is not an existing object in the universe that occupies time and space.
being (ens): A term for an existing thing.
The efficient cause answers the question, 'What or who made this object?' For example, the sculptor is the efficient cause of the statue.
Esse is a verb, representing the act of 'to be'.
The 'what-ness' of a thing.
The 'act of the being, being present'; the 'that-ness' of a thing.
The final cause answers the question, 'What is its purpose?' or 'What is the telos?' For example, the purpose of a statue is to memorialize an event.
The formal cause answers the question, 'How is it arranged?' What is its form? For example, arranging rock into a sculpture, distinct from a bench or a wall.
The scholastic phrase for God as 'the act of being itself'.
The material cause answers the question, 'What is it made of?' For example, a statue is made of stone.
Pantheism is the belief that the sum total of every existing thing is God.

entries.content: I watched this video where this guy explains how all of us have, like, some sort of causation loop... you keep asking, like, why am I here? And then you ask another why on top of that. And then at some point, it will lead to, like, a first cause of everything, which is apparently God.

God is the object of study in theology insofar as God is the focus of our discovery... But at the same time, God is also not an object. Because when you talk about an object, you're really referring to something that is literally right in front of you, something that you can define.

What's complicated about God is that we can't think with God precisely and say this, this, only this I'm going to study. Because when you're talking to a God, you're really talking about that ultimate reality.
Looking for God is not like a fish looking for the greatest, most powerful fish ever. It's more like the fish becoming aware of the water it is in.
God is not in the same category as the things a fish understands. God is somewhere outside of the way that this fish thinks.
If we're trying to understand what we mean by God, we have to get out of thinking that God is just some grand object existing in time and space.
God is not a being.
A 'being' (Latin: 'ens') refers to an existing thing or object in the universe that occupies time and space.
When Christians talk about God, we're not talking about a thing, because God is not a being.
Revelation. Faith everybody. Where we are currently at this point where we're discussing that God is not a being.
By being we mean that God is not an existing object in the universe that occupies time and space.
God is not like a chair, not like myself, not like anything that occupies any point in time and space in the universe.
Unlike the Greek gods, of course, who are representations of the volatility of the universe.
Bullivant makes clear in the reading that the most true God transcends natural abilities and does not require a beast.
Does God exist? The answer has to be properly nuanced. You have to clarify in what sense God does not exist, and in what sense God does exist.
Atheists are right in saying that God does not exist because there's no evidence of God. It's not as if you can pick up God's DNA.
The problem is that they often refer to God as just one more being (small letter 'b') in the universe.
God doesn't even belong to the genus of being. God doesn't belong to a category, a genus or a species.
God is not even the greatest of existing things or the greatest of all beings.

If you say that God is the greatest of all beings, he's still in the category of being. For example, if we say that Boris is the greatest student, that means Boris is part of that category of student. God isn't even in the category of existing things.
God exists, but not as one being among many. God doesn't belong to a category.
We can explain God's existence in two ways: 1) God enables existence to take place without being a being, and 2) God is both beyond and within our experience.
According to Aristotle, in order for you to know something, you have to be able to identify its four causes.
The material cause answers the question, 'What is it made of?' For example, a statue is made of stone.
The formal cause answers the question, 'How is it arranged?' What is its form? For example, arranging rock into a sculpture, distinct from a bench or a wall.
The efficient cause answers the question, 'What or who made this object?' For example, the sculptor is the efficient cause of the statue.
The final cause answers the question, 'What is its purpose?' or 'What is the telos?' For example, the purpose of a statue is to memorialize an event.
The principle is that every being has an efficient cause or a source from which it can account for its existence.
For Aristotle, you cannot create an entire chain of causes ad infinitum. At some point, you need to have a first cause that itself has no cause: an uncaused first cause.
Thomas Aquinas took Aristotle's philosophy and said that this first cause, the uncaused first cause, is what we call God.
The way that Aquinas puts it is that God is ipsum esse subsistens, the very act of being itself.
When 'Being' is capitalized, it is a verb. It is not 'ens' (a being, a noun) but 'esse' (the act of being, a verb). For Aquinas, existence is not a passive state, it's active.
Essence refers to what makes something what it is, its 'whatness'. It is related to the formal cause.
Existence refers to the act of a being being present; how an object manifests itself into the universe.
For Thomas Aquinas, what's interesting about God is that God's essence is existence.
When you talk about the essence of something, you say what it is and you say what it is not. Essence defines and therefore limits.
When you say that the essence of something is existence itself, that's unlimited. For Aquinas, God is the very act of being itself.
Everything exists as something, which implies a scope and a limit. Because you exist as this, you cannot exist as that.
What makes God unique (sui generis) is that because God is pure existence, God's nature is not limited. God doesn't exist *as* something; God just exists.
Asserting that God's essence is existence emphasizes God's non-competitive nature.

Beings tend to clash with each other; when one being asserts its power, the weaker is diminished. In contrast, when God asserts God's power, being isn't diminished, it flourishes. Creation takes place.
God's power isn't a threat to humanity. To think so (like some existentialist philosophers) is to hold an egoistic view of God, as a projection of the worst of humanity.
St. Augustine presents a tension in understanding God: God is both transcendental and immanent.
Transcendent means beyond our experience, outside of our categories, outside of our perception. God is completely other.
Islam and Judaism emphasize God's transcendence. In Islam, God is never depicted in art. For Jews, God's name (Yahweh) is not spoken.
To name something is to have some sort of control over it. The reluctance to name God respects his otherness.
Immanent means very much within our experience.
Pantheism is the belief that the sum total of every existing thing is God.
Christianity holds both transcendence and immanence. The Christian claim of the Incarnation (God entering the human experience) emphasizes God's immanence.

Analogy for transcendence/immanence: We may have never met artist Fernando Amorsolo (he is beyond our direct experience), but through his paintings, we can feel his presence (he is within our experience).
Being created in God's image should not be taken literally. It means we are able to create and relate like God.
Receiving Jesus' body as communion bread is an experience of God's immanence. Augustine says, 'You become what you receive.' It is communion, not consumption.
Augustine wrote of God: 'interior intimo meo et superior summo meo'—'More inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest thought.'
God can coexist in us because of God's non-competitive nature. If God is existence itself, and we share in that act of existence (in a limited way), then we are an offshoot of existence itself.
God is always loving. The proof is that the fact we still exist shows that God continues to share the gift of existence.

Not until I entered the Jesuits and began hearing about a different kind of God—a God who was with you in your suffering, a God who took a personal interest in your life, even if you didn't feel that all your problems were solved—did life started to make more sense.

entries.content: God, falsely so-called
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: A converse case can be made for arguing that it is the Judeo-Christian God who is falsely, or at least misleadingly, so-called. This is because Hellenized Jews stole the already existing Greek descriptive noun 'theos' as a name for Yahweh.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This is a point that the twentieth-century Anglo-Irish Dominican theologian Herbert McCabe was at pains to stress.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: We do not know what we are talking about when we use the word 'God' ... When Jews and Christians came to use the word 'god' it was already lying around meaning something else - I mean it meant something that God certainly could not be, a god. Whatever we are referring to when we use the word 'God' it can no more be a god than it can be a model aeroplane or half-past eleven. (Herbert McCabe, God Still Matters)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: 'God', 'Theos', 'Deus' is of course a name borrowed from paganism; we take it out of its proper context, where it is used for talking about the gods, and use it for our own purposes. This is quite a legitimate piece of borrowing and quite safe as long as it does not mislead us into thinking that the God we worship (or don't) is a god . . . He is always dressed verbally in second-hand clothes that don't fit him very well. (Herbert McCabe, God Still Matters)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: What McCabe is doing here is effectively turning Pseudo-Justin's argument on its head. He points out that, in its original context, 'god' (theos) denotes a class of personal, superhuman, supernatural beings, who collectively possess a wide range of impressive, though limited, super powers.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: But by choosing to refer to this One with the word 'god', Greek-speaking Jews - including, most influentially, those translating the Hebrew Scriptures - were using it as a name, rather than as a literal description. Transferred to this new context, the proper noun God became a kind of metaphor.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: There is a sense in which God is like a 'god' (he too is powerful, for example, albeit both far more so, and in a qualitatively different way), just as there is a sense in which God is like 'a mighty fortress' (he is steadfast, and protects those who 'dwell within' him). But the Judeo-Christian God cannot be an actual mighty fortress. And he cannot be, as McCabe reminds us, an actual 'god'.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: This point may perhaps be more easily grasped if one considers a couple giving their daughter the name of 'Poppy'. They may have many reasons for doing so... Feasibly, however, there is something about actual poppies that makes them think it would be a fitting name for a daughter: their beauty, or gracefulness, for example.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: They are not, of course, using this word as a straightforward description: she is not, and will not be, an actual member of the botanical family Papaveraceae. Instead, the word functions as a kind of metaphor or analogy: by naming her thus, her parents are intimating that they think (or hope) she will be like a poppy in some way.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: And this is, moreover, a point that applies far more widely... a strong case can be made that all human words about the Most True God are condemned to be egregiously lacking.
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: St Augustine's comment on our use of the word 'person'... as a description for the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit: 'Because the Father is not the Son... then certainly there are three... But when it is asked "Three what?" then the great poverty from which our language suffers becomes apparent. But the formula "three persons" has been coined, not in order to give a complete explanation by means of it, but in order that we might not be obliged to remain silent.' (On the Trinity, 5.10)
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: On arguably similar lines, the thirteenth-century German Dominican theologian Meister Eckhart writes in one of his vernacular sermons: 'let us pray to God that we may be free of God that we may gain the truth and enjoy it eternally.'
entries.sourceFile: R4 - Stephen Bullivant - THE 'ATHEISM' OF CHRISTIANITY.pdf

entries.content: One plausible interpretation... is that Eckhart is drawing a distinction here between the Most True God ('let us pray to God'), and our flawed human conceptions of who that God actually is ('that we may be free of "god"'), in the interests both of truth and salvation ('that we may gain the truth and enjoy it eternally').

entries.content: All human ideas about God must necessarily fall short of him. This is, as we have seen, the insight of such exemplary Christians thinkers as Augustine and Eckhart. This strand of Christian thought is sometimes called negative or apophatic theology.

topics.summary:
This topic challenges the common misconception of God as a 'being'—an object or entity existing within the universe alongside other things. The core lesson, drawn heavily from classical theologians like Thomas Aquinas, is that God is not a being (Latin: ens), but is Being itself (Latin: esse). This means God is the very act of existence, the uncaused first cause from which all created things (beings) derive their existence. While everything else exists *as* something specific and limited, God simply *is*. This distinction is crucial for understanding why traditional arguments for or against God's existence often miss the point; they typically search for God as if God were one more item in the catalogue of the universe, which is a category error.

This understanding of God as 'ipsum esse subsistens' (the very act of being itself) has profound implications. It establishes God as both transcendent (beyond our finite categories and full comprehension) and immanent (intimately present as the source of our own existence). The analogy of a fish in water is used to illustrate our existence within God's reality—God is not another fish, but the very medium that makes being a fish possible. This view also posits a non-competitive relationship between God and creation; because God is the source of being, God's power enables creatures to flourish rather than diminishing them.

Finally, the lesson emphasizes that all human language about God is necessarily analogical or symbolic. Theologians like Herbert McCabe highlight that even the word 'God' is a borrowed term, used metaphorically to name a reality that is fundamentally not a 'god' in the pagan sense of a limited, supernatural being. Our concepts and words fall short because they are designed for finite beings, not for the infinite act of Being itself. Therefore, faith and theology involve engaging with a mystery that can be pointed to but never fully defined, as Augustine suggests when he calls God 'more inward than my innermost self and higher than my highest thought.'
keyTakeaways: God is not a 'being' (ens) among other beings, but is 'Being itself' (esse)—the very act of existence.
For God, essence is existence. Unlike created things which have a separate 'whatness' and 'thatness', God's nature is to exist.
God is the Uncaused First Cause. Following Aristotle and Aquinas, the chain of causation must terminate in a source that does not itself require a cause.
All language about God is analogical or symbolic. Our finite words can only point to the infinite reality of God; they cannot contain or define it.
God is both transcendent (beyond our experience and categories) and immanent (intimately present within creation), a paradox best exemplified by the Incarnation.
studyTips: Master the distinction between 'ens' (a being, a noun) and 'esse' (the act of being, a verb). This is the central concept. Create a chart comparing God (as 'esse') to created things (as 'ens') on attributes like essence/existence, causality, and limitations.

Connect key thinkers to their core ideas: Aristotle (Four Causes, First Cause), Aquinas (Essence/Existence, 'ipsum esse subsistens'), Herbert McCabe (the word 'God' as a metaphorical name), and Augustine (God's immanence - 'interior intimo meo').

Practice explaining the analogies. Be prepared to articulate in your own words what the 'fish in the water' analogy reveals about God's immanence or what the 'Poppy' naming analogy reveals about religious language.

Focus on how this topic refutes common arguments. An exam might ask you to critique a statement like 'God is the most powerful being in the universe' or 'I don't believe in God because I can't see him.' Use the course concepts to explain why these statements are based on a flawed premise.

Memorize the definitions of Aristotle's Four Causes (Material, Formal, Efficient, Final). Pay special attention to the 'Efficient Cause' as it directly leads to the argument for God as the 'Uncaused First Cause'.

connections.explanation: This topic directly corrects distorted images of God (e.g., as a cosmic vending machine or a competitor) by presenting a more abstract and profound theological understanding of God as Being itself, which is non-competitive and not an object.

The idea of 'Christian Atheism' aligns with this lesson's rejection of a false 'god'. A Christian 'atheist' denies the existence of a 'god' who is merely another being in the universe, which is precisely the point Herbert McCabe makes in order to affirm the true God who is beyond that category.

God as 'esse' frames God as the ultimate 'Mystery' to be participated in, not a 'Problem' to be solved with empirical data. The quote 'If you understand, it's not God' directly links these two topics, showing that God's nature transcends finite human problem-solving.

If God is transcendent and our language is inadequate, the topic of 'Revelation' explains how we can know anything about God at all. Revelation is God's self-communication, bridging the gap between the infinite 'esse' and finite human beings.

quizQuestions.question: According to Thomas Aquinas, what is the fundamental difference between God and a created thing like a chair?
choices: A) For the chair, its essence and existence are distinct; for God, His essence is His existence.
B) God has a final cause, whereas a chair does not.
C) God is composed of a more perfect material cause than the chair.
D) The chair exists in space, while God exists only in time.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core of Aquinas's argument is that created things (beings, 'ens') are composed of an essence ('what' it is) and the act of existence ('that' it is). For God alone, these are identical: God's very nature or essence is 'to be' ('esse').

quizQuestions.question: When Herbert McCabe says that the word 'God' is like 'second-hand clothes that don't fit him very well', he means that:
choices: A) The term was borrowed from paganism and is used metaphorically to name a reality that is not a 'god' in the pagan sense.
B) Theologians should invent a new, more fitting word for God.
C) The original Hebrew name for God, Yahweh, is a more accurate description.
D) God's true name has been lost and 'God' is a poor substitute.
quizQuestions.explanation: McCabe's point is that the word 'theos' (god) originally referred to a class of powerful-but-limited beings. By using it as a proper name for Yahweh, early Greek-speaking Jews and Christians were using it analogically, not literally, to point to something beyond that original meaning.

quizQuestions.question: The argument that any chain of causation must lead back to a 'first cause that itself has no cause' is used to establish God as the:
choices: A) Uncaused First Cause
B) Final Cause
C) Material Cause
D) Pantheistic All
quizQuestions.explanation: This is the classic cosmological argument, associated with Aristotle and Aquinas. It posits that an infinite regress of efficient causes is impossible, so there must be an initial, uncaused source of all causation, which is identified as God.

quizQuestions.question: Why is the statement 'God is the greatest of all beings' considered theologically inaccurate in this course?
choices: A) It places God within the category of 'being', even if at the top, when God is not in any category at all.
B) It implies a competition, and God is non-competitive.
C) It is an example of pantheism, equating God with the best part of creation.
D) It overemphasizes God's transcendence at the expense of immanence.
quizQuestions.explanation: The key lesson is that God doesn't belong to the genus of 'being' (`ens`). To call God the 'greatest being' is like calling the author the 'greatest character' in their novel; it fundamentally misunderstands the author's relationship to the characters as their creator, not their peer.

quizQuestions.question: The analogy of a fish becoming aware of the water it is in illustrates:
choices: A) The shift from looking for God as an object ('another fish') to recognizing God as the all-encompassing reality in which we exist.
B) The idea that God is a fluid and ever-changing being.
C) The concept of God as the final cause or 'telos' of humanity.
D) The impossibility of ever truly understanding God's nature.
quizQuestions.explanation: The analogy contrasts the incorrect search for God as a specific object ('the greatest fish') with the correct awareness of God as the immanent ground of all being ('the water'), which is everywhere but often unnoticed.

quizQuestions.question: Which of these concepts primarily emphasizes God's immanence?
choices: A) The Incarnation
B) God as the Uncaused Cause
C) Islamic prohibitions on depicting God in art
D) The idea that 'if you understand, it's not God'
quizQuestions.explanation: The Incarnation, God entering the human experience in the person of Jesus, is the ultimate Christian claim of God's immanence—God's presence 'within' our world and experience. The other options tend to emphasize God's transcendence.

quizQuestions.question: A sculptor who carves a statue from a block of marble is an example of which of Aristotle's Four Causes?
choices: A) Efficient Cause
B) Material Cause
C) Formal Cause
D) Final Cause
quizQuestions.explanation: The efficient cause is the agent or 'who' that brings something into being. The sculptor is the agent that acts upon the marble (material cause) to give it the shape of the statue (formal cause) for a specific purpose (final cause).

quizQuestions.question: The concept of a 'non-competitive' understanding of God means that:
choices: A) The more powerful God is, the more human beings can flourish.
B) God does not interfere with human free will.
C) God is equal in power to other spiritual forces.
D) God cannot be compared to anything else.
quizQuestions.explanation: Unlike the dynamic between two beings (`ens`), where one's assertion of power can diminish the other, the relationship between God (`esse`) and a creature is different. Since God is the source of being, God's power gives being and allows the creature to become more fully itself.

quizQuestions.question: What is the meaning of the scholastic phrase 'ipsum esse subsistens'?
choices: A) The very act of being itself
B) God is the highest thought
C) Everything has an efficient cause
D) A being is a being
quizQuestions.explanation: 'Ipsum esse subsistens' is the Latin phrase used by Aquinas to define God. It literally translates to 'subsistent being itself,' meaning God is not a thing that *has* existence, but is the pure act of existence itself.

quizQuestions.question: According to Paul Tillich, why must 'man's ultimate concern' be expressed symbolically?
choices: A) Because the ultimate (God) infinitely transcends the realm of finite reality, which cannot express it directly.
B) Because symbolic language is intentionally vague and open to multiple interpretations.
C) Because religious founders preferred storytelling and myth over direct statements.
D) Because symbols are more emotionally powerful than literal statements.
quizQuestions.explanation: Tillich's argument is that since God (the ultimate) is infinite and our reality and language are finite, no finite thing or word can properly and directly express the infinite. At best, the finite can only point to the infinite symbolically.

entries.content:
It's like the story of the atheist caught in a flood. The fellow figures that the flood threatening his house is the chance to prove conclusively whether God exists. So he says to himself, If there is a God, I will ask him for help, and he will save me. When he hears a warning on the radio advising listeners to move to higher ground, he ignores it. If there is a God, he will save me, he thinks. Next, a firefighter knocks on his door to warn him to evacuate. “If there is a God, he will save me,” he says to the firefighter. When the floodwaters rise, the man climbs to the second floor. The coast guard boat motors by his window and offers him rescue. “If there is a God, he will save me,” he says and refuses help from the coast guard. Finally, he ends up on the roof, with the waters rising around him. A police helicopter hovers over the house and drops a rope to climb. “If there is a God, he will save me!" he shouts over the roar of the helicopter's blades. Suddenly a giant wave sweeps over him, and the man drowns and finds himself in heaven. When God comes to welcome him, the atheist is first surprised. And then furious. “Why didn't you save me?” he asks.

“What do you mean?” says God. “I sent the firefighter, the coast guard, and the police officer, and you still wouldn't listen!"

entries.content: Experiencing God also comes through personal interactions within the community. Sure, God communicates through private, intimate moments—as in prayer or reading of sacred texts— but God also enters into relationships with us through others in a faith community. Finding God often happens in the midst of a community—with a 'we' as often as an 'I.' For many people this community is a church, a synagogue, or a mosque. Or more broadly, religion.

revelation & faith
Revelation and faith can be approached from three angles: 1. from an experiential angle, 2. from a theological angle, and 3. from a philosophical angle.

topics.summary:
This topic explores 'Revelation' as the core concept of how God communicates with humanity. Theology, defined as the study or meaningful discussion (*logos*) of God (*theos*), is fundamentally an attempt to understand and articulate this divine self-communication. The course materials emphasize that revelation is not merely a transfer of information, concepts, or images, but a personal and relational event. It's the process by which God, who is ultimately a mystery beyond our full comprehension ('If you understand, then it is not God'), makes Himself known and accessible.

Revelation is presented as occurring through two complementary modes: 'Deeds' (God's actions in history) and 'Words' (the explanation and expression of those deeds). A key example of missing revelation is the story of the atheist in the flood, who fails to recognize God's help because it doesn't match his preconceived expectations. This illustrates that we must be open to how God actually communicates, often through ordinary means and people.

Crucially, the Christian tradition claims that the ultimate and most complete revelation is the Incarnation—God becoming human in Jesus Christ, who St. Paul calls 'the image of the invisible God.' This event makes the invisible God visible and accessible within the human condition. Finally, experiencing this revelation is not a purely individualistic affair. While private moments of prayer and study are important, God's communication is frequently encountered within a community of faith, where we experience God through our relationships with others.
keyTakeaways: Revelation is God's self-communication, a relational event rather than a transfer of abstract concepts or data.
Revelation occurs through two complementary modes: 'Deeds' (God's actions in history) and 'Words' (the interpretation of those actions).
For Christianity, the Incarnation of Jesus is the ultimate and fullest form of God's revelation, making the 'invisible God' visible and accessible.
Revelation is experienced both personally (e.g., prayer) and communally. The community of faith is a primary context for encountering God.
Divine revelation points to a mystery that cannot be fully captured by human language or concepts, as expressed by the phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus' (If you understand, it is not God).
studyTips: Focus on the 'how' of revelation. Be prepared to explain the complementary modes of 'Deeds' and 'Words' using a concrete example, like the Exodus story if it was mentioned in class.

Memorize and be able to explain the significance of the quote 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus'. Connect it to the ideas of 'Mystery' and why doctrines are considered 'imperfect attempts' to articulate God's revelation.

Use the story of the atheist in the flood as a key case study. Be ready to analyze it on an exam to explain how preconceived notions or 'Distorted Images of God' can cause one to miss God's communication in everyday life.

Master the connection between Revelation and the Incarnation. A likely exam question will be: 'How does the Incarnation represent the climax of revelation in Christianity?' Your answer should reference Jesus as the 'image of the invisible God.'

Practice contrasting personal ('I') and communal ('we') experiences of God. Be able to argue why the course emphasizes the community as a vital place for God's revelation, using the provided course notes.

connections.explanation: Revelation does not provide a solution to God as a 'problem,' but invites us into a relationship with God as a 'mystery.' Our doctrines are our attempts to articulate this inexhaustible mystery, not solve it.
Faith is the human response to God's initiative of Revelation. Revelation is God's self-communication, and faith is the act of trusting and entering into that communication.

Having distorted images of God can prevent us from recognizing His revelation when it occurs. The atheist in the flood story missed God's help because his image of a saving God didn't include firefighters and coast guards.

The idea that revelation points to a mystery we can't fully grasp ('Si comprehendis, non est Deus') aligns with this topic. Revelation communicates a reality that transcends our limited human category of 'a being' among other beings.

quizQuestions.question: According to the course material, what is the primary definition of 'Revelation'?
choices: A) A set of secret doctrines given to the church.
B) God's way of communicating Himself to humanity.
C) The final book of the New Testament.
D) A purely personal and private spiritual experience.
quizQuestions.explanation: The core lesson defines Revelation simply as 'how God communicates' or 'God's way of communicating'. It is presented as a broad act of self-communication, not just secret doctrines (A), a single book (C), or a purely private event (D).

quizQuestions.question: The story of the atheist in the flood primarily illustrates which concept about revelation?
choices: A) God only saves those who are part of an organized religion.
B) Miracles are the only true form of revelation.
C) Preconceived expectations can cause us to miss God's communication in ordinary events.
D) God's power is limited by natural disasters.
quizQuestions.explanation: The man in the story had a specific, miraculous idea of how God should save him and therefore rejected the real help God sent through a firefighter, a boat, and a helicopter. This shows how our expectations can blind us to revelation happening in everyday life.

quizQuestions.question: What is the meaning of the Latin phrase 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus'?
choices: A) If you have faith, you will understand God.
B) If you can understand it, it is not God.
C) Only through comprehension can one find God.
D) God is incomprehensible, therefore unknowable.
quizQuestions.explanation: This quote from St. Augustine means 'If you understand, it is not God.' It emphasizes that any concept of God that we can fully grasp or define with our minds is, by definition, too small to be the true God, who remains a mystery.

quizQuestions.question: In the Christian tradition, what is considered the fullest and most complete form of God's revelation?
choices: A) The writing of sacred texts like the Bible.
B) The creation of the world.
C) The Incarnation of Jesus Christ.
D) Personal prayer and mystical experiences.
quizQuestions.explanation: The course materials state that Christianity claims God can be seen and imaged because of the Incarnation. St. Paul is quoted as saying Jesus is the 'image of the invisible God,' making the Incarnation the ultimate act of God's self-revelation.

quizQuestions.question: Revelation is said to occur through two complementary modes. What are they?
choices: A) Private thought and public worship.
B) Miracles and visions.
C) Scripture and tradition.
D) Deeds (demonstration) and Words (expression).
quizQuestions.explanation: The 'DISTINCTIONS' section explicitly states that 'Revelation occurs through two complementary modes: Deeds (demonstration) and Words (expression).' This means God acts in history, and also provides the words to interpret those actions.

quizQuestions.question: How does the course's emphasis on community relate to the topic of Revelation?
choices: A) Revelation can only happen within the walls of a church, synagogue, or mosque.
B) God often communicates and is experienced through our relationships with others in a faith community.
C) Individual experiences of God are considered less valid than communal ones.
D) The community's role is to dictate the one correct interpretation of revelation.
quizQuestions.explanation: The notes state that 'Finding God often happens in the midst of a community—with a 'we' as often as an 'I.'' This highlights that relationships with others are a key medium through which God's revelation is encountered, complementing private experiences.

quizQuestions.question: Based on the definition of 'theology' (theos + logos), what is its primary activity?
choices: A) Proving the existence of God through logic.
B) Engaging in meaningful discussion and study about God.
C) Following a set of divine laws without question.
D) Achieving a state of absolute certainty about divine matters.
quizQuestions.explanation: Theology comes from 'theos' (God) and 'logos' (study, talk, order, meaning). The course materials define 'logos' as a 'meaningful discussion about a certain topic.' Therefore, theology is the study of or meaningful discussion about God.

quizQuestions.question: Plato's Allegory of the Cave is used as an illustration in this course to suggest that:
choices: A) God is like a shadow on a cave wall.
B) Education and revelation can help us move from seeing shadowy images to a truer reality.
C) The physical world is an evil prison we must escape.
D) Only philosophers can truly understand God.
quizQuestions.explanation: The allegory describes moving beyond 'shadowy images to see the world as it really is.' In the context of theology and revelation, this serves as an analogy for moving beyond incomplete or distorted ideas to a fuller understanding of God's reality.

quizQuestions.question: Why does the course material state that doctrines are 'imperfect attempts at articulating a mystery'?
choices: A) Because doctrines are outdated and need to be replaced by modern speculation.
B) Because God is fundamentally a mystery that exceeds the capacity of human language to fully capture.
C) Because the original authors of doctrine were not intelligent enough.
D) Because all doctrines are equally valid, making them all imperfect.
quizQuestions.explanation: This ties directly to 'Si comprehendis, non est Deus.' If God is an infinite mystery, then our finite human language and concepts (doctrines) can only point towards that mystery; they can never contain or exhaust it. They are attempts, not final definitions.

quizQuestions.question: The distinction between 'knowing about' someone and 'knowing them personally' is used to emphasize that revelation is primarily:
choices: A) An intellectual and academic pursuit.
B) A collection of historical facts and data.
C) A list of moral rules to be followed.
D) A relational and personal encounter with God.
quizQuestions.explanation: This distinction highlights the difference between having factual knowledge (knowing 'about') and having a relationship (knowing 'personally'). The course uses this to argue that God's revelation is not just a list of facts about Him, but an invitation into a personal relationship.
